column34: We used three “Robot Zone 22mm 16:1 Planetary Gearmotor 730rpm” motors for each robot to enable movement. When four motors are used on uneven ground, one wheel may fail to make full contact with the floor, causing unstable operation. By using three motors, however, all wheels remain in contact with the surface, allowing the robot to move smoothly and reliably.
column35: We installed a “TAU-0826B 6V” solenoid and designed it to operate at 12V, enabling it to strike the ball with greater force. This setup allows for stronger and more effective performance compared to running it at the nominal 6V.
column36: We used a “Chihai GM25-370 4.4:1 5000rpm” motor and built it with a 2:1 belt drive system, allowing the ball to spin at approximately 10,000rpm. This design enables higher rotational speed and more effective ball control

column52: The robot's position was determined using a camera, and the arena was divided into 12 zones so that the robot would operate differently depending on which zone it was in.
column54: IR Seeker (ST-3811) was placed in 12 directions, and it was determined that the ball was located in the direction where the largest value was detected.
column55: When the ball is located behind, it is made to rotate around the ball, and when it is in front, the distance to the ball is determined based on the IR value. As the ball approaches, the speed is decelerated to prevent the ball from bouncing out of the capturing zone.
column56: Using a camera, the ground was divided into 12 zones, and the movement speed and movement method were implemented separately depending on the current zone.
column57: In addition, it automatically controls the speed based on the location detected by the camera to minimize collisions with walls, and ensures that it does not go outside the playing field by moving to the center of the field when a line is detected.
column58: When the ball is within the capturing zone and in our territory, the robot is made to move quickly toward the opponent's goal and attempt a shot at a specific location and time using a Solenoid. Additionally, if the opposing robot interferes with the robot's movement and there is no movement for more than 3 seconds, a turning shot is performed.
column59: A separate function was implemented for the goalkeeper to find an appropriate position based on the distance from the ball and obstruct the goal, and to quickly knock the ball away when it is close to the goalkeeper or to block the path so that the opposing robot cannot approach.
column60: Each robot moves independently without communication with other robots.

column52:
LED Dynamic Driving: 
We use 24 phototransistors for line detection. We use 24 WHITE LEDs as light sources. Each LED consumes 20mA. If we turn on 24 LEDs at the same time, 480mA of current will flow. We used two TLC6C5912, 12-bit Shift Register LED drivers to limit the number of LEDs that can be turned on at the same time. Currently, only 4 LEDs are turned on at the same time. If we are reading one sensor now, we only turn on the LEDs up to the 3rd sensor to be read.
column54: The ball is basically detected by the camera through the reflector.
column55: When the robot detects the ball with the camera, the robot draws a virtual circle on the ball, finds a point where the robot and the circle meet, moves to that point, and when it reaches the starting position, moves to the back of the robot along that virtual circle. If the ball is in front, it moves to the back of the ball.
column56: We used BNO055 sensor. We control only the direction.
column57: Boundaries are detected using light sensors. Determine the direction of travel through logical operations and move in the opposite direction.
column58: Using coordinates collected through the camera and mirror, approach the ball from behind, turn toward the goal, and shoot.
column59: Use the angle between the center of the goal and the ball to move the robot along that line and block the ball.
column60: The robots communicate with each other. The attack robot sends an ACK (0x064) every 0.2 seconds. If the defense robot does not receive this signal for more than 1 second, it considers the attack robot to have been kicked out and switches to attack immediately. If this signal is received again, it finishes what it was doing and switches back to defense mode.
column61:
Escaping from the line:
The robot has 16 sensors placed at equal intervals on a circle. When a line is detected, the average of the physical coordinates of the sensors defined by the robot is calculated. This value becomes the vector value of the direction in which the robot was moving. We move the robot in the opposite direction of this vector until the sensors that passed the line cross the line again.

column32: We designed the mechanical parts using Fusion to model the robot structure, casing, and supports. We considered the internal space, motor position, 120° omni-wheel layout, sensors, cameras, and future upgrades. We also tried using AI for 3D design, but it did not work well for our case, so we improved the design manually.
column33: We built the robot with custom PCBs designed in EasyEDA and mechanical parts modeled in Fusion. The PCBs were externally manufactured, then soldered and assembled at IITA. During tests, we rewired one motor and fixed sensors.
column34: We used 3 brushed DC “TT” 5 V motors per robot, 6 in total, with Pololu 30:1 HP #1093 as reference. This matches our KIWI base with 3 omni wheels at 120°, allowing forward, sideways and rotational movement with less weight and complexity than 4 motors. We used commercial omni wheels and no kicker/dribbler motors.

column38: Our main mechanical innovation was reducing the robot size so it could fit within the competition limits, decreasing its diameter by about 1.5 cm. We also designed a modular and detachable structure in two parts: an upper perception/communication module and a lower movement/decision module. This makes assembly, repairs, testing, and future upgrades easier.
column41:
¿Cómo funcionan sus circuitos de alimentación?
Our robot uses a 2S LiPo battery, 7.4 V nominal and 8.4 V fully charged. TOP and DOWN use Schottky diodes for polarity protection and MP1584 buck converters to generate 5 V for logic and 3.3 V for sensors. CENTRAL uses the Zircon board regulation.
column42: The CENTRAL Teensy 4.1 drives 3 H-bridges on the Zircon board. Each motor uses INA/INB pins for direction and an 8-bit PWM signal, 0–255, for speed. PID and omni-3 kinematics control each wheel independently.
column43: We use four processors: TOP has a Teensy 4.0 for cameras, IMUs and distance sensors; CENTRAL has a Teensy 4.1 on the Zircon board for strategy, PID, omni-3 kinematics and motors; DOWN has a Teensy 4.0 for line sensors and odometry; COMM has an ESP32-C6 for the RCJ referee/communication. We chose this to split tasks and process data close to each sensor.
column44: The CENTRAL Teensy 4.1 calculates the robot movement using the game strategy, PID controllers and omni-3 kinematics. It converts the desired movement into three wheel speeds, then sends direction signals and PWM values to the Zircon H-bridge drivers, which power each motor independently.
column45: Our robot uses two OpenMV N6 cameras, one facing forward and one facing backward. Each camera runs a vision script that detects the orange ball using color thresholds and blob detection. The camera sends the ball angle, position, visibility and confidence to the TOP board. TOP filters and combines this data into a WorldSnapshot, which is sent to CENTRAL. Then CENTRAL uses it in the game strategy to search, approach, align with the goal and push the ball.
column46: The DOWN board uses 32 ALS-PT19 phototransistors with 0402 LEDs to detect the white line on the green carpet. Four CD4051 multiplexers send the analog readings to the Teensy 4.0, which filters them and sends line status to CENTRAL.
column47: We use 4 VL53L7CX ToF sensors, 2 BNO055 IMUs, 2 SparkFun OTOS sensors and 32 line sensors. ToF and IMUs connect to the TOP Teensy 4.0 by I2C. OTOS and line sensors connect to the DOWN Teensy 4.0 through I2C and CD4051 multiplexers. ToF/OTOS help estimate position, and BNO055 IMUs give the robot heading.
column48: The RCJ referee command arrives by BLE to the COMM ESP32-C6 board. COMM outputs 3.3 V START/STOP GPIO signals through a TXS0102 level shifter. TOP reads pins 5/6 with pulldown; HIGH means play, both LOW means stop or cable fail.

column52:
Our main electronic innovation was designing our own TOP and DOWN boards, using AI as a learning tool and EasyEDA for PCB design. The TOP board handles perception: cameras, IMUs, ToF sensors, ultrasonic sensor and referee input. The DOWN board handles line sensors and optical odometry. Both send processed data to the CENTRAL board, which makes decisions and controls the motors.

We are also proud of the distributed architecture: instead of using a single board, we use specialized boards that process data close to each sensor. This reduces data traffic, makes testing easier, and allows us to improve or replace one module without redesigning the whole robot.

Another important part was solving real integration problems: separating I2C buses to avoid conflicts, fixing ToF sensor reading, using multiplexers to read 32 line sensors, and adapting the RCJ referee system through GPIO. It was not just wiring components; we had to measure, test, fix, and validate each part on the bench.
column54: The ball is located with two OpenMV N6 cameras, one front and one rear. Each camera detects the orange ball using color thresholds and blob detection, then sends visibility, relative position and confidence by UART to the TOP board. TOP filters and combines the data into the WorldSnapshot, which CENTRAL uses to approach and push the ball.
column55: Our algorithm depends on the robot role. The attacker searches for the ball, moves behind it, aligns with the opponent goal and pushes it by inertia. The goalkeeper stays near our goal, patrols laterally, follows or predicts the ball position, intercepts shots and clears them. Both use the same WorldSnapshot data, but different strategies.
column56: We use BNO055 IMUs for heading, VL53L7CX ToF sensors for wall distance, SparkFun OTOS for optical odometry, and line sensors for boundaries. TOP and DOWN preprocess the data and send it to CENTRAL, which uses position and heading to correct motion, patrol, approach the ball, and avoid lines.
column57: The robot detects field lines with 32 ALS-PT19 phototransistors and LEDs on the DOWN board. Four CD4051 multiplexers send the analog readings to the Teensy 4.0. The software filters the values, detects white-line direction and urgency, then CENTRAL stops or moves away to avoid leaving the field.
column58: Our scoring algorithm uses camera data to find the ball and opponent goal. The attacker moves behind the ball, aligns itself with the goal, and pushes the ball by inertia. We do not use a kicker or dribbler in this version, so there are no extra motors or driver circuits for them. This reduced weight and complexity.
column59: Our defense algorithm is used by the goalkeeper. It stays near our goal and patrols laterally instead of chasing the ball everywhere. Using WorldSnapshot data, it follows or predicts the ball position, intercepts shots toward our goal, and clears the ball away. Line avoidance has priority.
column60: Our robots do not currently communicate with each other during the match. However, the system was designed with this feature planned for future development. The idea is to use communication to share information such as ball position, robot role, and game state, so the attacker and goalkeeper can coordinate better.
column61: Our software innovations include robust multi-board UART communication with CRC16 and sequence numbers, camera-based ball position/distance estimation, TOP-side sensor fusion with prediction to fill gaps between readings, Claude-assisted programming validated with tests, and monitoring software for bench testing.
column64: Final robot components: USD 6,000 total, about USD 3,000 per robot. Trial and error: ~USD 6,000. Environment/tools: ~USD 3,000. These are Argentina landed costs, including import taxes, customs and shipping. Rate: 1 USD = 1 USD.

column15: Our mentor helped us with the decision of components, usage of Raspberry Pi, improvement of electrical circuits and correction of PCBs.
column16: WhatsApp and Discord were used for communication, GitHub was used for backup and code sharing, and for schedule management we used Notion and Obsidian.
column17: ChatGPT and Gemini were used to get a better understanding of mathematical concepts and code algorithms.
column32:
Fusion 360 was used to design the mechanical parts. The project combines methods used by international teams and methods developed by our own.
The robot is designed in a conical format to increase the used area without creating blind spots. For quick repairs and an easier assembly process, we included a lid on the bottom that gives access to the internal area of the robot.
We changed from PLA and ABS to PETG filament, that has a higher value of tensile strength, being ideal to absorb impact.
column33:
Supports were produced using a Bambu Lab A1 3D printer, and the printed circuit boards (PCBs) of copper and glass fiber were manufactured by JLCPCBs.
The PCBs were specifically designed to fit components, with cuts for motors, dribbler and battery.
column34:
Four GM4108 motors allow an omnidirectional movement. In a graph of sine and cosine of an angle the incerceptation is in 45°, so between motors 90° is ideal. To include a dribbler and minimize the impact on movement, the angle was increased to 100°.
Four omniwheels are mounted with two flat parts, one with hooks to lock the subwheels wire and one with cavities for the hooks, reducing weak points.
column35: A small JF-0826B solenoid of 6V and 20N powered with 40V allows a stronger yet smaller kicker, occupying less space in the center of the structure. A PETG pin is connected to the solenoid plunger and was designed to pass through the dribbler. Its curved shape maximizes contact area with the ball.
column36: The mechanism uses two pulleys and a belt, connecting the motor to the roller with a 1:1 relation, producing less surface damage than using gears. The silicone disks of A55 shore are sanded to increase grip and have a curve to improve its contact area with the ball, focusing in control precision.
column38: To reduce the width of the dribbler, keep using the pulleys and belt system and maximize the surface area of the silicone disks, it was created a design where the pulleys enter partially in the dribbler support, with one of the silicone disks having an internal curve where the dribbler support enters to hold the roller bearing. This way, the silicone sizes were preserved and the dribbler width was reduced, decreasing the angle change of the motors and reducing the impact on the robot's movement.
column41: The robot uses a 12.6V, 2.2 Ah LiPo battery instead of 1.1Ah considering new higher power demand components. Converters provide 20V for the motors, 48V for the kicker, 5V for the Raspberry Pi and 3V3 for the bottom LEDs. External energy input enables battery replacement without restarting the robot.
column42: To drive the motors we use Field-Oriented Control algorithm with Simple FOC mini module, that translate the signal from microcontroller to Step-Up voltage, maximizing force.
column43: There are two Raspberry Pico 2 as microcontrollers, one being responsible for reading most of the sensors and running the main code of the robot, and the other being responsible only for the motors. It is dual core and can be programmed in Arduino IDE, an improvement when compared to the previous controller, a STM32F405. The camera processor used in the robot is the Raspberry Pi 5 of 8GB, one of the options with the higher processing capacity and RAM, allowing more ideal FPS and image quality.
column44: The processor defines the forces needed to move in x and y axis and robot's angle. Using trigonometry, the forces are divided and sent to each motor. AS5600 absolute encoders are used to maintain the FOC algorithms and read the angular position of the motor by detecting magnetic field variations.
column45: To detect the ball on the field there are two cameras with 1.4mm fisheye lens that achieve 210° of Field Of View (FOV) horizontally and 150° vertically. The distance between cameras creates a blind spot that allows wires and supports to be passed without harming the vision of the field. The FOV allows only one camera to be used per time to detect the ball, reducing processing. The dribbler also uses a laser and a phototransistor to detect precisely when the ball is in position in the dribbler.
column46: There are 16 color sensors, each one composed of a red LED and a phototransistor. 4 Analogic to Digital Converters (ADCs) of model ADS1015 are used, each one reading four sensors. The ADCs use I2C communication, so only 2 pins, SDA and SCL, are used to send the readings to the main board.
column47: To obtain the angle of the robot in relation to an offset and keep track of the direction the robot is facing, there is a BNO055 sensor. It is possible to use a MPU6050 gyroscope as a second option if there are problems with the BNO, being a more familiar but older option of component. Eight VL53L1X LiDARs are used to detect distances to the walls, used to calculate the robot's position.
column48: The circuit is separated from the main board for safety, receives 12.6V from the battery and uses a XL6009 to convert it to 40V. When the processor sends a signal, the charging of the 6 2200uF capacitors is cut and the voltage is sent to the solenoid, with two mosfets preventing energy reversion.
column49: The dribbler uses an A2212 1400KV motor, meaning it can go up to 17640 RPM when powered with 12.6V, but we limit it to 20-40%, that is already enough for its use. To control the motor there is an ESC of 30A. We use a trimpot to calibrate the  sensitivity of the ball detecting system phototransistor.
column52:
The kicker circuit uses a system of recharge and shoot to reduce the activation time and increase the shooting power. It communicates to the main board by sending a signal when the capacitors are charged and receiving a signal when to shoot. By creating a shooting logic, it is possible to minimize the activation time needed for the kicker.
To measure the charge, there is a comparator that reads the capacitors tension, and to create the logical condition there is a transistor that activates when the controller emits charge, deactivating the circuit responsible for charging the capacitors and at the same time activating the one that releases the charge and triggers the solenoid.
column54: The robot uses both cameras, searching for the orange threshold calibrated for the ball using HSV values. The camera that detects the ball becomes the main one and gets the ball angle in relation to the image center and its distance to the robot by relating pixels to centimeters. The data is combined with the robot's angle and position to determine the ball position on the field.
column55: The striker goes to the ball based on a function that relates the current ball angle read to predefined angles of movement, so the robot makes the most efficient trajectory to catch the ball. Approaching the ball, the striker slows down and activates the dribbler. The goalkeeper uses predefined positions around its own goal, going to each position based on the trajectory estimation of the ball.
column56: The robot's position allows movements to specific points on the field and considers an average of two calculations. One uses the 8 LiDARs and the robot's angle, getting the LiDAR rays that are closer to being perpendicular to the four walls, so they reflect back properly. The other uses the camera images, combining the angle and distance to one of the goals with the field size and robot's angle.
column57: The robot uses the 16 color sensors disposed in a smaller circle, that allows the robot to cross approximately 5 cm beyond the white lines, increasing the area of possible movement on the field without risks of hitting the walls. We calibrate the code to detect the white lines, and with trigonometry it is possible to find its angle in relation to the robot, that goes to the opposite direction.
column58: To score goals, the striker mainly relies on the movement function to get to the ball, the precision dribbler to catch and hide the ball, the kicker to shoot, and the ball and robot positions achieved with sensors. The strategy focuses on stealing and hiding the ball next to the field lines and ensuring an ideal position to shoot directly or with a curve, depending on the opponents behavior.
column59: To avoid the opponent team scoring, one of our robots is focused on defense. It uses the ball positions in a time interval to predict its trajectory and find, considering the position points we defined around our own goal, the ideal position to defend. Considering the rules, if the goalkeeper gets close to the ball, it uses the dribbler to catch it and changes to attack mode for a time.
column60: The robots communicate with each other using the HC-05 or the RCJ bluetooth modules. With it, the robots can send information during the match. By sharing the ball position and sending the striker to the other side of the field, it is possible to counter robots that hide the ball, and by sending the robots own position and specific commands, it is possible to create more dynamic strategies.
column61: With the FOV and cameras’ placement, most of the time only one camera is used in full quality to detect the ball, while the other one is either deactivated or used in low quality. This way, it is possible to dedicate the processing usage to only one camera. An algorithm is used to keep track of the ball angle, changing which camera is used when the ball is leaving the cameras FOV.

column15: Our mentor assisted to debug electrical issues with the kicker PCB and our Out of bounds algorithm with the light sensors.
column16: By communicating via Teams, WhatsApp, and Discord, and using to-do lists and work logs, we managed our workload efficiently! This consistent workflow helps track our robot's progress, saves time, and cuts out redundant tasks.
column17: We use Gemini for coding practices, field strategies, and docs, and Claude Code to design an aesthetic, secure website.
column32: Using Autodesk Fusion 360, we upgraded our previous robot by adding a kicker, dribbler, and custom dual-layer omni wheels. To offset the added weight, we optimised existing components to minimise mass while ensuring structural strength. Key changes included designing a compact, high-stability wheel hub and heavily modifying the internal layout to fit the new mechanical systems alongside the PCBs and motors.
column33: We manufacture parts using Bambu Lab 3D printers (PLA/ABS) and a CNC miller for 3mm aluminium plates. PCBs are ordered from JLCPCB, with components mainly sourced via Digikey. PCBs were hand soldered.
column34: The robot features an X-shaped drive system with four 3D-printed-bracket-mounted Maxon DCX19 motors for omnidirectional movement without rotation. Overvolted at 12V for extra speed and torque, they power custom dual-layer omni wheels. These wheels use durable ABS frames and silicone-moulded rollers on metal cores, maximising traction, surface contact, and stability during competitive play.
column35: Our kicker uses a modified TAKAHA CB1037 solenoid with a custom 250-turn coil and a lightweight 3D-printed casing. The standard tip was replaced with a horizontal bar for reliable ball contact. Powered via a voltage booster that jumps 12V to 48V, it delivers a significantly stronger kick.
column36: Our dribbler uses a 3D-printed housing with a 1:4 speed-multiplying gear train, running the motor efficiently while spinning the roller at high velocities to save power. The roller features silicone molded around a 3D-printed rod to maximize grip and prevent ball slippage in the capture zone.
column38:
Our most significant innovation was a custom wheel hub, inspired by previous models, designed to attach our dual-layered omni wheels. This integration achieved better traction on the slippery felt field.

The hub directly modifies our existing design by adding a specialised internal groove for a square nut. This allows a bolt to tighten directly onto the motor's D-shaft, ensuring a secure mechanical connection that eliminates the risk of the wheel sliding off the axle.
column41: A 12.6V LiPo battery powers the motors and kicker directly. Buck converters step this down to 5V (for the Teensys, camera, Bluetooth, and photogate) and 3.3V (for the IMU, IR sensors, and light ring), ensuring a stable, regulated power supply across all components.
column42: The robot uses five VNH7070 motor drivers powered directly by the battery. Four drivers control the drive system, receiving PWM commands from dual Teensy 4.1 microcontrollers to regulate each motor's speed and direction, enabling precise and powerful omnidirectional movement.
column43: We use two Teensy 4.1 microcontrollers. One MCU reads the 24 TSSP sensors and communicates the ball data to the primary MCU via UART. The primary MCU reads the light sensors, IMU, receives the ball data and drives the motors and kicker.
column44: Exit VNH7070 standby: hold InA and InB HIGH for at least 20us. To drive, set one LOW, the other HIGH, while modulating PWM for speed. Both HIGH locks the motor; both LOW coasts. To coordinate, the processor uses a cosine function to calculate exact speed and direction for each motor.
column45: Our system detects a 40kHz IR ball using TSSP77038TR sensors. The ball's 24% duty cycle is polled over a 10ms window to locate it. To ensure stability, an Exponential Moving Average (EMA) filter smooths the data and remove high frequency noise on the readings. We then treat each value as a vector and sum them to determine the ball direction and strength.
column46: The robot has 48 LEDs/phototransistors. These are multiplexed using 3x 16 channel multiplexers for the primary MCU to read. 32 sensors are arranged in a ring and the other 16 are placed on the edge of the robot for early line detection.
column47: Connected to a Teensy processor, our robot uses an OpenMV N6 Camera via UART for absolute positioning, calculating coordinates using trigonometry on blue and yellow goals. For heading, a BNO055 sensor connects via I2C. By setting a baseline orientation upon startup or software reset, it continuously tracks our true direction relative to the field, ensuring dynamically accurate movement.
column48: A 12V supply is boosted to 48V to charge two parallel 4700μF capacitors. The microcontroller monitors this charge via a voltage divider. Upon a command, two N-channel MOSFETs discharge the energy through the solenoid to kick. A manual button routes power through a 50Ω resistor to safely de-energize.
column49: Our dribbler system is a fifth Maxon motor which its circuit is integrated into our main board and is powered the same as the drive motors.
column52:
A key innovation in our design is the custom dual-ring light sensor board, which provides a fast and reliable line avoidance system. The board utilizes a total of 48 sensors arranged in two concentric rings: an outer ring of 32 sensors and an inner ring of 16 sensors. 

The 32 sensors on the outer ring are divided into four distinct directional zones (front, back, left, and right) to provide rapid boundary detection and early warning alerts. The inner ring of 16 sensors serves as the primary trigger, signalling the robot to execute immediate corrective manoeuvres to remain within bounds. 

Integrating all 48 sensors into a single, consolidated printed circuit board (PCB) significantly reduces internal wiring. This layout minimizes the risk of loose connections and simplifies troubleshooting by allowing for rapid fault isolation.
column54: To locate the ball, our robot rapidly polls 24 IR sensors over a 20-millisecond window to count detected light pulses. These raw counts are normalised against the ball's duty cycle and smoothed by an EMA filter to prevent erratic spikes. Vector summation then resolves the exact relative angle. The magnitude is scaled to 0-100% for distance and sent via UART to the processor.
column55: Our two-Teensy system uses a secondary Teensy to read IR sensors, pinpointing the ball's angle and distance without a camera. The primary Teensy uses this data to steer a smooth, curved orbit behind the ball, slowing for sharp turns and surging forward when perfectly aligned. This fluid approach replaces an older, twitchy method that ignored distance and glitched across the center line.
column56: Using a camera to spot coloured goals, the robot calculates distance by size and notes the angle. Combining this with a digital compass, it pinpoints exact field coordinates. It navigates by plotting a straight line to the target, smoothly setting speed and direction. When the ball is off-field, this system centers the robot, ensuring the fastest route to the next ball.
column57: Our robot multiplexes 48 light sensors, using dynamic calibration to isolate white lines. A Center of Mass algorithm generates a raw line vector from these active sensors. This vector is compass-corrected for field relativity and processed by a state machine tracking crossing depth. Finally, a PID controller uses this data to dynamically scale recovery speed in the exact opposite direction.
column58: To score efficiently, our software merges orbit and goal-tracking algorithms using TSSP sensors and a camera. The orbit algorithm calculates an offset to position the robot behind the ball. Upon contact, an active dribbler firmly secures it, preventing wasted kicks and enabling passes. Using camera data, the robot dynamically aligns with the goal and triggers a powerful, calculated kick.
column59: Our defender uses three PID controllers. A rotational PID keeps the robot facing away from the goal. The vertical PID controls depth via line sensors, using camera distance if the line is lost. The horizontal PID centres the robot, or laterally mirrors the ball to block shots. Combining these outputs into one resultant vector ensures fluid, omnidirectional movement to protect the goal.
column60: The robots communicate via bluetooth assigning roles based on a 100-point score. The ball proximity (40pts), front attack cone (20), goal alignment (15), defensive distance (15), and battery health (10) are the factors the algorithm will consider. The robot with the highest score attacks. If one robot is taken off, then the remaining robot defends the goal.
column61: Our proudest innovation is a multi-stage IR sensor pipeline to track a 40kHz pulsed ball. We rapidly poll 24 TSSP77038TR sensors 500 times per 10ms window. To prevent jitter, an EMA filter blends this with historical data, halving standard deviation to 0.47. A vector summation calculates exact relative angles, while raw magnitude (110-130) is scaled to a 0-100% value. This information is sent to the main controller to determine motor movements.

column16: We communicated through a WhatsApp group and used Google Drive to store documents, which allowed us to assign and share tasks easily between team members.
column17: We’ve used Claude and Gemini to assist code debugging and in the process of creating a good written documentation.
column32: We made our design entirely in Fusion360 to make 2D and 3D models. We came up with this final design after searching for international teams strategies and mechanical structure. We faced many challenges, like the ball capturing zone (1.5 cm) and the adaptation to the new maximum weight. In comparison to the last competition, our robot now uses the TSSP4038 and the BNO055. This change demanded a new design for the TPU holders and with the creation of a similar chassis basis made in Policarbonate.
column33: The Kobra S1 3D printer and the Duplotech 6040 for 3D and 2D parts,using PC and TPU filament and acrylic plates. Instead of 3D printing, our omniwheels are made of aluminum by GTF Robotics. Our PCBs were designed in EasyEDA and printed by JLCPCB.
column34: We used 4x JGA25-370 620 RPM. At the beginning of the season we used 25GA-370, but due to the lack of RPM(280) we decided to buy a new motor. Lastly, we decided to use 4 motors per robot because by adding just 1 motor the robot can become stronger, faster and it can lead to better navigation too.
column35: N/A
column36: N/A
column38: The mechanical part we are most proud of is the IR PCB Support. This new support allowed us to use the TSSP4038 to its best way by using small boxes with a centered fissure enveloped with aluminium, which is responsible for limiting the angle at which the ball is read. That way, by attaching these boxes in each one of the 16 spaces in the “crown of IR”, we can have a solid read of the ball.
column41: Our robot uses a 11.1V battery pack which is connected to two switches in parallel. One of them activates the microcontroller, while the other turns on the motors. Before getting in the ESP32-S3, the voltage is regulated to 5V through a buck converter.
column42: The motors are driven through a L293D IC. Each IC is capable of controlling the speed and direction of two motors, so we use two of them to drive our four motors. Besides being powered by the battery voltage, this component is attached directly to the PCB.
column43: There are 4 ESP32-S3 microcontrollers. One of the processors is located in the Main PCB, responsible for controlling the motor through two L293D ICs and processing the data from BNO055 (compass). The second one is in charge of processing the data from each of the IR sensors’ readings. The third microcontroller processes the ultrasonics’ data and the last one interprets the reflectance of the field based on the fluctuation of resistance in the LDRs.
column44: The L293D IC has 6 digital inputs, 3 responsible for controlling each motor. Two of them control the direction of the motor: if LOW/LOW or HIGH/HIGH, the motor is turned off; if HIGH/LOW or LOW HIGH, the motor activates. The third pin controls the speed of the motor, by receiving an 8-bit value.
column45: Our ball detection system consists of a PCB with 16 TSSP4038 IR sensors. There’s a 22.5° gap between each, organized in a round pattern. They identify the angle of the ball relative to the robot. However, we cannot read the intensity of the signal with them, so we also use a NXT Infrared Seeker for this purpose. If we didn’t, it wouldn’t be possible to know how close the ball is. As a result, our robot is able to have a ball detection system with 360° coverage and to read intensity as well.
column46: It consists of 5 LDRs and 4 LEDs. The light from the LEDs is reflected by the field: white reflects more light than green. The reflected light reaches the LDRs and their resistance varies. The microcontroller measures the voltage difference and confirms the presence of a line.
column47: We use a BNO055 connected via I2C to the Main microcontroller in order to measure the direction that the robot is facing. To find our position on the field we use 3 ultrasound sensors (US-015) located in the right, left and front.
column48: N/A
column49: N/A
column52: The part that we are most proud of is definitely our Infrared PCB. We have been effectively working on it since 2025, with a lot of attempts. In the beginning of our production, we used a NXT Infrared Seeker to detect the ball. It was easy to work with, but it had a lot of inconsistencies and even a 120° blind spot. Last year, we tried using a multiplexer to connect 16 IR sensors, but some would occasionally stop reading and it had a bit of latency. This year, we’ve abandoned the multiplexer and connected 16 TSSP4038 IR sensors directly to the ESP32-S3, in a round pattern. The data from the sensor readings is sent to the Main PCB through a wireless protocol called ESP-NOW. As a result, the Main PCB decides which way to go based on the direction of the ball.
column54:
Our microcontroller  performs a sampling of 200 digital readings from the 16 sensors, summing the values into a variable every time a HIGH signal is detected. We then calculate the ball's direction by applying a vector sum based on the angles of the sensors and the "strength" of the detections.
The process is handled by a dedicated ESP32-S3. After that, the data is transmitted to the main board.
column55:
For our attacker, we execute an "orbit" maneuver. It calculates the shortest trajectory that curves behind the target, placing the robot smoothly behind the ball. This is achieved by adding or subtracting the IR sensor values from the motor powers via a vector sum.
Our defender's movement is strictly lateral, maintaining a blocking position directly in front of the ball within the goal area.
column56:
We determine the individual velocity of each motor by decomposing the desired trajectory vector. We then feed orientation data from a BNO055 compass into a Proportional-Derivative (PD) controller.This generates a correction signal that is applied to the individual motor velocities. 
Furthermore, we determine our absolute position on the field by using ultrasonic sensors.
column57: To prevent the striker from leaving the field, we use 3 ultrasonic sensors. When these sensors detect a distance below the set threshold, the robot maneuvers away from the wall. Furthermore, to avoid entering the goal area, we use an array of five LDRs at the front of the robot. If any of these sensors detect a line, the robot immediately moves backward.
column58:
We rely purely on the kinematic pushing force of our attacker.
Our goal-scoring algorithm consists of 3 main objectives: verifying our field limits via ultrasonic readings, adjusting our motor power via the compass PD controller to keep the robot constantly facing the opponent's goal, and executing our orbit maneuver to get behind the ball and continuously drive it forward until the play is reset.
column59: Our defensive strategy is built upon 3 foundational pillars. The defender verifies its position strictly within our designated field limits using the ultrasonic sensors. Then, it applies the compass-driven PD controller to its motors to correct them and maintain a strict forward-facing alignment. Finally, it acts as a blocker, mirroring the ball's lateral movement to intercept shots.
column60: Yes, the robots communicate via the ESP-NOW protocol, allowing the defender to constantly monitor the striker's status on the field. If the striker goes out of bounds (or becomes inactive), the defender dynamically switches to the striker's behavioral logic. This strategy ensures we maintain offensive pressure and prevents the opponent from driving the ball toward our goal.
column61: The algorithm we are most proud of is our ball detection logic. We had been working with the TSSP4038 sensors for quite some time, but initially struggled to achieve peak efficiency. We sought inspiration from top-tier world championship teams utilizing the same hardware (such as Munako Aegis). By thoroughly analyzing their public documentation, we expanded our knowledge base and engineered a software solution capable of tracking and pursuing the ball with high efficiency.

We faced many challenges, like the ball capturing zone (1.5 cm) and the adaptation to the new maximum weight. In comparison to the last competition, our robot now uses the TSSP4038 and the BNO055. This change demanded a new design for the TPU holders and with the creation of a similar chassis basis made in Policarbonate.
column33: The Kobra S1 3D printer and the Duplotech 6040 for 3D and 2D parts,using PC and TPU filament and acrylic plates. Instead of 3D printing, our omniwheels are made of aluminum by GTF Robotics. Our PCBs were designed in EasyEDA and printed by JLCPCB.
column34: We used 4x JGA25-370 620 RPM. At the beginning of the season we used 25GA-370, but due to the lack of RPM(280) we decided to buy a new motor. Lastly, we decided to use 4 motors per robot because by adding just 1 motor the robot can become stronger, faster and it can lead to better navigation too.
column35: N/A
column36: N/A
column38: The mechanical part we are most proud of is the IR PCB Support. This new support allowed us to use the TSSP4038 to its best way by using small boxes with a centered fissure enveloped with aluminium, which is responsible for limiting the angle at which the ball is read. That way, by attaching these boxes in each one of the 16 spaces in the “crown of IR”, we can have a solid read of the ball.
column41: Our robot uses a 11.1V battery pack which is connected to two switches in parallel. One of them activates the microcontroller, while the other turns on the motors. Before getting in the ESP32-S3, the voltage is regulated to 5V through a buck converter.
column42: The motors are driven through a L293D IC. Each IC is capable of controlling the speed and direction of two motors, so we use two of them to drive our four motors. Besides being powered by the battery voltage, this component is attached directly to the PCB.
column43: There are 4 ESP32-S3 microcontrollers. One of the processors is located in the Main PCB, responsible for controlling the motor through two L293D ICs and processing the data from BNO055 (compass). The second one is in charge of processing the data from each of the IR sensors’ readings. The third microcontroller processes the ultrasonics’ data and the last one interprets the reflectance of the field based on the fluctuation of resistance in the LDRs.
column44: The L293D IC has 6 digital inputs, 3 responsible for controlling each motor. Two of them control the direction of the motor: if LOW/LOW or HIGH/HIGH, the motor is turned off; if HIGH/LOW or LOW HIGH, the motor activates. The third pin controls the speed of the motor, by receiving an 8-bit value.
column45: Our ball detection system consists of a PCB with 16 TSSP4038 IR sensors. There’s a 22.5° gap between each, organized in a round pattern. They identify the angle of the ball relative to the robot. However, we cannot read the intensity of the signal with them, so we also use a NXT Infrared Seeker for this purpose. If we didn’t, it wouldn’t be possible to know how close the ball is. As a result, our robot is able to have a ball detection system with 360° coverage and to read intensity as well.
column46: It consists of 5 LDRs and 4 LEDs. The light from the LEDs is reflected by the field: white reflects more light than green. The reflected light reaches the LDRs and their resistance varies. The microcontroller measures the voltage difference and confirms the presence of a line.
column47: We use a BNO055 connected via I2C to the Main microcontroller in order to measure the direction that the robot is facing. To find our position on the field we use 3 ultrasound sensors (US-015) located in the right, left and front.
column48: N/A
column49: N/A
column52: The part that we are most proud of is definitely our Infrared PCB. We have been effectively working on it since 2025, with a lot of attempts. In the beginning of our production, we used a NXT Infrared Seeker to detect the ball. It was easy to work with, but it had a lot of inconsistencies and even a 120° blind spot. Last year, we tried using a multiplexer to connect 16 IR sensors, but some would occasionally stop reading and it had a bit of latency. This year, we’ve abandoned the multiplexer and connected 16 TSSP4038 IR sensors directly to the ESP32-S3, in a round pattern. The data from the sensor readings is sent to the Main PCB through a wireless protocol called ESP-NOW. As a result, the Main PCB decides which way to go based on the direction of the ball.
column54:
Our microcontroller  performs a sampling of 200 digital readings from the 16 sensors, summing the values into a variable every time a HIGH signal is detected. We then calculate the ball's direction by applying a vector sum based on the angles of the sensors and the "strength" of the detections.
The process is handled by a dedicated ESP32-S3. After that, the data is transmitted to the main board.
column55:
For our attacker, we execute an "orbit" maneuver. It calculates the shortest trajectory that curves behind the target, placing the robot smoothly behind the ball. This is achieved by adding or subtracting the IR sensor values from the motor powers via a vector sum.
Our defender's movement is strictly lateral, maintaining a blocking position directly in front of the ball within the goal area.
column56:
We determine the individual velocity of each motor by decomposing the desired trajectory vector. We then feed orientation data from a BNO055 compass into a Proportional-Derivative (PD) controller.This generates a correction signal that is applied to the individual motor velocities. 
Furthermore, we determine our absolute position on the field by using ultrasonic sensors.
column57: To prevent the striker from leaving the field, we use 3 ultrasonic sensors. When these sensors detect a distance below the set threshold, the robot maneuvers away from the wall. Furthermore, to avoid entering the goal area, we use an array of five LDRs at the front of the robot. If any of these sensors detect a line, the robot immediately moves backward.
column58:
We rely purely on the kinematic pushing force of our attacker.
Our goal-scoring algorithm consists of 3 main objectives: verifying our field limits via ultrasonic readings, adjusting our motor power via the compass PD controller to keep the robot constantly facing the opponent's goal, and executing our orbit maneuver to get behind the ball and continuously drive it forward until the play is reset.
column59: Our defensive strategy is built upon 3 foundational pillars. The defender verifies its position strictly within our designated field limits using the ultrasonic sensors. Then, it applies the compass-driven PD controller to its motors to correct them and maintain a strict forward-facing alignment. Finally, it acts as a blocker, mirroring the ball's lateral movement to intercept shots.
column60: Yes, the robots communicate via the ESP-NOW protocol, allowing the defender to constantly monitor the striker's status on the field. If the striker goes out of bounds (or becomes inactive), the defender dynamically switches to the striker's behavioral logic. This strategy ensures we maintain offensive pressure and prevents the opponent from driving the ball toward our goal.
column61: The algorithm we are most proud of is our ball detection logic. We had been working with the TSSP4038 sensors for quite some time, but initially struggled to achieve peak efficiency. We sought inspiration from top-tier world championship teams utilizing the same hardware (such as Munako Aegis). By thoroughly analyzing their public documentation, we expanded our knowledge base and engineered a software solution capable of tracking and pursuing the ball with high efficiency.

column11: We met twice a week for approximately 3 hours per meeting (about 6 hours per week in total).
column12: We worked in our high school's robotics workshop.
column14: Our team has participated in several robotics competitions over the past few years, including Iran Open 2024 (Open League), Iran Open 2025 (Open League), RoboCup 2025 (Open League), and Iran Open 2026 (Open League). These competitions helped us improve our technical skills and gain valuable experience.
column15: Our mentor supervised the project and mainly helped with Raspberry Pi–ATmega2560 communication and Raspberry Pi power issues.
column16: We managed the workload using a Telegram group with topic-based threads. Tasks were assigned to team members based on their roles, and progress was tracked through regular updates, discussions, and task reports within the group.
column17: We used AI as a teacher for AI Hat and Hailo-8L, to understand CV docs, and to speed up debugging and improve code quality.
column32: Using SolidWorks for 3D mechanical design over RoboCup seasons, we replaced the mechanical kicker with a solenoid system and redesigned the motor layout to widen the robot's front opening. This year, we focused on compact space optimization due to new components and designed a modular structure to simplify assembly and maintenance. We used an iterative process of prototyping, testing, and redesign until reaching the final optimized design.
column33: We used 3D printing for mechanical parts and assembled them in-house. PCBs and the mirror system were produced by local companies. During assembly, we modified the design to fix fit issues and widened the intake for better ball handling.
column34: We use four GTD-35 brushless motors for omnidirectional movement. Their angles were optimized (improved from a previous 45° layout) to increase maneuverability and intake performance. We also adjusted the front motor angles to widen the ball intake and upgraded the dribbler motor for higher speed and better ball control.
column35: We replaced our mechanical kicker with a solenoid-based system for higher power and reliability. It is controlled by a custom PCB. Due to its large size, we redesigned the internal layout to integrate it efficiently within the robot.
column36: Our dribbler uses a silicone roller driven by a ZGA25 motor through 3D-printed gears. We upgraded from a 500 RPM motor to a 1000 RPM motor and changed the gear ratio to increase roller speed, improving ball control, retention, and dribbling performance.
column38: Our main mechanical innovation is a redesigned solenoid kicker system. We replaced the previous mechanical kicker with a solenoid to increase shot power, speed, and reliability. The mounting structure was redesigned to ensure precise alignment with the center of the ball for consistent impact. The internal layout was also optimized to fit the solenoid while maintaining compactness and structural stability, improving overall scoring performance.
column41: Our robot uses a 3-cell LiPo battery. Motors are powered directly from the battery with a protection circuit. A SKMW30F-05 buck converter provides stable 5V for electronics. The Raspberry Pi is powered via a USB PD 3.0 module with 5V 3A output for safe and reliable operation.
column42: Our motors are powered by 12V and have built-in drivers using RS-485 communication. Since RS-485 is not UART-compatible, we use an SN65LBC184DR transceiver to convert UART to differential signals, enabling reliable and noise-immune motor control.
column43: We initially used the Arduino Mega 2560 because it met our I/O and functional requirements. However, its large size made integration difficult in our compact robot. Therefore, we switched to using the ATmega2560 microcontroller directly on a custom PCB, which reduced space usage and allowed us to fully customize the hardware layout for our system.
column44: We control motors using a continuous 0–360° movement system. The processor calculates the desired angle and uses trigonometry to set each motor’s speed. This replaces our previous 32-direction system and enables smoother omnidirectional movement, improving accuracy in tracking and positioning.
column45:
We replaced Pixy CMUCam5 with a Raspberry Pi and Camera Module 3 due to low frame rate and unreliable data. We chose Pi over OpenMV and OV5640 for flexibility and processing power, and explored an AI Hat for faster object detection.

The Pi processes camera images to detect the ball and sends position data to the ATmega2560, which controls motor movement. We use a mirror-based 360° view and tested a second front camera, but the final system uses a single optimized vision pipeline.
column46: We use LDR sensors in a pull-up circuit for line detection. Each LDR is connected through a multiplexer to the ADC, allowing sequential reading. This improves accuracy and lets us determine exactly which part of the robot crosses the boundary line for better control.
column47: We use the TDAxis12 sensor module (MPU6050 + BNO055) for orientation and field direction using sensor fusion. It is connected via I2C to the processor. A TD53L1 distance sensor is used for positioning on the field. Together, these sensors provide robot orientation, position, and support role-based navigation for attackers and defenders.
column48: Our kicker uses a solenoid powered by an XL6009 boost converter with capacitor banks for peak current. An IRLZ44N MOSFET switches the solenoid via microcontroller control. A flyback diode protects the circuit from voltage spikes during turn-off, ensuring safe and reliable operation.
column49: Our dribbler system uses a DRV8874 motor driver controlled by PWM signals from the microcontroller. This allows precise speed control of the motor, resulting in smooth and stable rotation of the dribbler for consistent ball handling and possession during matches.
column52:
One of our key electronics innovations is splitting the system into multiple dedicated PCBs due to space limitations inside the robot. This modular design also improves reliability by separating high-power circuits, such as the 48V kicker and drive system, from sensitive control and processing electronics, reducing electrical noise interference.
We addressed the challenge of inter-board communication by designing robust connectors and well-structured interfaces, ensuring stable and reliable data and power transfer between all modules.
column54: When the camera detects the ball, the Raspberry Pi calculates its X and Y coordinates and distance. This data is sent as a serial packet to the ATmega2560, which uses it to determine the ball’s position relative to the robot and adjust movement accordingly. We also tested a second camera to reduce blind spots, but currently use a single optimized camera system.
column55: We use a single camera with a mirror for 360° vision, dividing the field into 32 sectors. The ball’s X,Y position is mapped to a sector, and the robot moves accordingly to bring the ball in front for capture. Both robots use the same algorithm, with identical behavior for approaching and collecting the ball.
column56: We use distance sensors (front, back, left, right) and an IMU to estimate position and orientation. Distance values are compared to detect walls and field zones, while the IMU ensures correct heading. If misaligned, the robot first corrects its angle, then re-evaluates distances. Based on this fused data, it adjusts direction and position to stay inside the field and navigate to target zones.
column57: We use LED-LDR pairs to detect boundary lines. Each LDR is connected individually for precise detection. To reduce processor load, LDRs are only read when a distance sensor detects proximity to the field edge. When sensor values cross a calibrated threshold, the robot immediately moves away from the detected side to stay inside the field.
column58: We use three scoring algorithms: Direct Shot, Back Shot, and Square Shot. The robot selects the best strategy based on ball position, obstacles, and field location. The dribbler maintains ball possession while maneuvering, and the solenoid kicker is activated when the robot reaches a favorable shooting position, maximizing scoring opportunities.
column59: Our robots communicate with each other and dynamically switch roles. When an opponent approaches our goal with the ball or takes a shot, the defending robot immediately detects the threat, switches to an attacking role, and moves at maximum speed to intercept or capture the ball before a scoring opportunity develops.
column60: We use HC-05 Bluetooth modules for robot-to-robot communication. Each robot shares ball detection status and position to enable dynamic role assignment. If both see the ball, the closer robot becomes attacker; if only one sees it, it becomes attacker; if neither sees it, both switch to positioning mode to search and maintain field coverage.
column61: One of our main software innovations is a dynamic goal-scoring state machine. The robot analyzes ball and goal positions, obstacle distances, field position, and orientation to select the best scoring strategy in real time. By calculating relative angles between the robot, ball, and goal, it can perform advanced tactics such as back shots and angle-based scoring, improving adaptability and increasing scoring opportunities during matches.

column32:
We designed all mechanical components with SolidWorks. We adopted a 4-camera and lidar fusion solution for football recognition and positioning, with a special wheel layout of 90° rear wheels and 120° front wheels.

During the design phase, we fully considered structural strength and manufacturing cost. We matched different materials including PLA, PETG and steel to achieve the best balance. We also arranged all cables neatly on both sides of the chassis to ensure convenient daily maintenance.
column33:
We primarily use the Bambu Lab X1C 3D printer to manufacture most of the components of the robot.

Our PCBs are manufactured by JLCPCB.

The gears and skeleton structure are produced by an external company through metal 3D printing.
column34:
We used four motors, chosen for the following key reasons:

1. Enhanced Power and Stability
- Four motors provide greater combined torque, enabling the robot to maintain stable movement during collisions with opponents.

List of drive system components:
1. motor - Model: RoboMaster M2006
- Q: 4
2. wheel:
- Q: 4
- Main wheel: petg

3. Connecting parts
column35: We fully designed the mechanical structure of our  kicker in SolidWorks. The whole assembly consists of a U-shaped outer frame, a bottom cover, a central coil bobbin, copper winding coils, a moving core, a static core and an ejection push rod.
column36:
We designed the whole dribbler mechanical structure in SolidWorks. 
 
Two bearings are installed at both ends of the roller shaft to reduce friction and ensure smooth rotation. After determining all transmission parts, we sketched and extruded the main dribbler frame .
column38:
In the design of robotic mechanical systems, our proudest innovation is the reverse hub-motor embedding design.

After finalizing the motor and kicker components, the wheel design faced a critical challenge: the motor we selected has a long shaft with an outer-biased fixing position. If this conventional scheme were followed, it would cause dimensional overrun.

we shift the wheel's fastening position from the inner side to the outer side, moving the wheel inward toward the motor.
column41:
The robot’s main power source is a 6S high-discharge lithium battery with a capacity of 1450mAh:
1. 12V step-down circuit exclusively supplies power to the dribbler motor.

2.  5V regulator circuit power for all 5V-rated components, including MaixCam, Lidar, and Teensy 4.1 MCU.
column42:
- Our MCU is Teensy 4.1 with native CAN peripheral. It connects to a 3.3V-powered Adafruit CAN Pal transceiver.
- The module contains an internal boost circuit to output standard 5V CAN bus level for chassis motor ESCs.
column43: We choose PJRC Teensy 4.1 as our main controller because its 600 MHz Cortex-M7 high-speed processor provides powerful computing performance for real-time processing tasks; it features 8 hardware UART ports, abundant GPIO pins and native CAN interface, which can connect our camera, radar, gyroscope, OLED and motor CAN bus simultaneously without extra expansion chips, fully meeting the robot’s multi-module control requirements.
column44:
Teensy 4.1 collects data from sensor. Combined with our match strategy, it calculates target speed, moving direction and angular velocity of the  robot.

We substitute the parameters into equations. MCU conducts trigonometric solving to split the overall movement into independent target speed .
column45: Four cameras mounted circumferentially (front/right/rear/left), each covers 90° with 15° overlap for 360° blind-free vision. Each runs 100Hz onboard HSV blob detection, outputs bounding box center & size via separate UARTs to Teensy 4.1. Main controller converts pixel to global angle & distance per calibrated offset, selects closest-to-center camera when multi-detected. Microswitch on dribbler senses ball contact via GPIO.
column46: Our robot has no line detection board. We uniquely rely solely on LiDAR distance data to determine robot position relative to field white lines for our out-of-bounds algorithm. This coordinate-based monitoring system triggers immediate corrective action when nearing restricted areas.
column47: For navigation, we use YDLIDAR T Mini Plus for field coordinates and HWT101 gyroscope for heading, both via serial ports to Teensy 4.1. Four onboard cameras detect goal frame position for aiming before shooting. Gyroscope provides real-time attitude feedback, enabling directional adjustments based on match strategy.
column48: Our 24V electromagnetic kicker uses MOT1112T MOSFET with push-pull gate drive for fast switching. Optocoupler isolates Teensy 4.1 from high-power loop. Schottky diode absorbs reverse EMF to protect MOSFET. Aluminum substrate dissipates heat; series fuse cuts power on short circuit.
column49: Our dribbler uses a brushless motor with ESC, controlled by Teensy 4.1 PWM for speed regulation to hold the ball. A microswitch on the frame detects ball possession; Teensy reads its logic level to confirm clamping state in real time.
column52:
We are most proud of our custom low‑voltage high‑current electromagnet kicking system.
 
We started by testing and rewinding commercial electromagnets and found their performance could be greatly improved. From experiments, we concluded a key design trade-off: more wire turns increase magnetic force but raise resistance and lower current, which suits high-voltage driving. Under competition voltage limits, a low-voltage large-current design became our optimal development direction.
 
A 48V capacitor discharge scheme was unsuitable for our robot. If we wanted to reach 48V while maintaining high output current, we would need extremely large capacitors that cannot fit inside our limited chassis space. Direct drive with the original 6S battery voltage works much better. Batteries carry ample energy and provide strong instantaneous power output, so we specially chose high-rate lithium cells for this system. We also calculated the total power consumption of all other robot modules to allocate enough instantaneous current for the electromagnet.
 
Within a fixed coil volume, we adopted rectangular flat copper wire. Unlike regular round wire, flat wire improves space utilization, allows more winding turns and strengthens magnetic flux. We also replaced ordinary iron with DT4C high-purity soft iron for the core to achieve faster magnetization and lower hysteresis loss.
 
Mechanically, we proposed a pre-travel acceleration optimization: letting the armature move through a short accelerating stroke before striking the ball produces a faster kick. We verified this improvement with our self-built laser speed measurement device.
 
On the drive circuit side, we selected high-current MOSFETs to match our power demand, yet these chips come with a large parasitic input capacitance of 14000 pF. In comparison, small-signal low-current MOSFETs only have parasitic capacitance of several tens of picofarads. Without a high-power dedicated driver, the gate voltage VGS would take far too long to rise, slowing down the electromagnet’s response severely. To solve this issue, we designed a push-pull drive circuit to accelerate gate charging and switching speed.
 
After multiple design iterations and trial-and-error tests, our first self-made electromagnet solution is fully functional and ready for practical use. However, we have several potential improvement ideas that remain untested. We will carry out further experiments to verify these optimization directions in future work.
column54: Every 10ms, Teensy 4.1 reads ball bounding box data from 4 cameras via UART, computes pixel center. Local angle = (horizontal center - 224)/224 × 45°, then adds 90°/180°/270° per camera for global angle, normalized to 0-360°. Distance = vertical center/6.24 minus per-camera shift offset; clamp ≤0 to 1. Selects camera with smallest angular offset as final position. Microswitch confirms ball grip.
column55:
Our team assigns strikers and defenders with separate algorithms. Strikers use two modes:

 (1) Tangential orbit approach—solves right triangle using ball distance as hypotenuse and virtual circle radius as leg to compute tangent offset. 

(2) Direct head-on tracking—locks robot facing ball with speed scaled by distance. 

The defender stopped on the line connecting the goal and the ball.
column56: LiDAR scans field, filters noise, then runs horizontal/vertical voting to identify  boundary lines with highest scores. From these lines, algorithm computes robot's X-Y absolute coordinates and heading angle for full-field localization. When no ball detected, robot returns to standby positions. When ball secured, robot combines coordinates with LiDAR data to follow fixed attacking trajectories.
column57: Our OOB algorithm uses LiDAR  data plus velocity decomposition. Radar scans boundaries to establish real-time coordinate vectors. For straight sidelines, decompose velocity into tangential + normal, cancel outward normal component. For curved penalty areas, cancel radial component pushing outward. Recombine remaining vectors to keep robot in-bounds while preserving motion toward ball.
column58:
Goal-scoring: After ball capture, robot uses radar for goal angle + gyroscope for orientation. When aligned within tolerance, triggers kicker for direct shot. Alternative: radar-guided waypoint navigation along sideline to opponent goal line, then aligns and shoots. Both strategies confirm alignment via radar/gyroscope before kicker activation.
For an visual picture, please refer to the doc .
column59:
Defense uses velocity decomposition to circle penalty zone. Radar adjusts radial velocity to maintain circular path, facing outward. Splits ball-directed velocity into components to control goal distance, recombines for precision. Positions on ball-goal line to block; stops when aligned. Clears stationary ball by turning and shooting toward opponent goal.
For an picture, please refer to the doc .
column60:
Two robots communicate via HC-05 Bluetooth) with Teensy 4.1 over uart, exchanging XY coordinates and strategy flags. 
Benefits:
 (1) Avoid collisions—share positions to attenuate boundary-directed vectors when close.
 (2) Eliminate ball conflicts—assign ball priority in penalty zone; non-priority robot avoids area.
column61: We achieve LiDAR-based full-field localization: point cloud data fused with gyroscope for correction, horizontal/vertical voting extracts top 4 line segments as field boundaries. LiDAR also detects enemy robot coordinates. Two robots share status via Bluetooth; filtering out teammate point cloud interference ensures stable enemy localization.

column32: We designed the robot using SolidWorks, developing initial concepts through internal ideation and refining them using resources such as YouTube and designs from other teams. These concepts were regularly discussed and evaluated during team meetings with our mentors. We iteratively improved key components, including the shooting mechanism and wheel design. Additionally, we developed a mechanical filter for the TSSP sensor to effectively reduce noise and enhance signal reliability.
column33: We manufactured parts using a Bambu Lab 3D printer, optimizing slicing settings for strength and accuracy. Components were tested and refined, improving chassis, wheels, and shooting system while adjusting tolerances for reliable overall performance.
column34: We used four brushless motors with eight wheels, with two wheels mounted on each motor, along with one motor for the shooter and one for the spin-back mechanism. The four-motor drivetrain enables omnidirectional movement and precise control across the field. The wheels were custom-designed in SolidWorks, inspired by omni-wheel concepts, ensuring smooth motion and stability.
column35: The kicker mechanism was designed in SolidWorks and 3D printed using a Bambu Lab printer with PLA material. For the first time we make kicker with spring and linear gear system , combined with a worm gear to provide torque and control, ensuring consistent and reliable kicking performance.
column36: The dribbler was designed in SolidWorks as a rotating cylindrical roller at the front of the robot. When the ball enters the dribbler area, it captures and controls it. The mechanism is powered by a DJI2212 920KV motor, providing sufficient speed and torque for consistent ball handling.
column38: Our key mechanical innovations include a custom wheel design optimized through extensive testing to achieve smooth, stable, and efficient movement across the field. We also developed an advanced dribbler capable of capturing balls even from corner positions without entering them, improving control and flexibility during play. Additionally, our kicker is not like others adn compeletly its new and its our biggest innovation in our robot,It is carefully work with the dribbler for better performance
column41: Our robot uses a 12.6V battery (3 cell) as the main power source. This is regulated to 5V and 3.3V using voltage regulators to power components such as the TD-Axis, ESP32, STM32, and TSSP sensors,and line detection sensors ensuring stable and reliable operation.
column42: The motors are driven using RS485 protocol from the STM32 microcontroller to control speed.However the GTD35 driver is on it and  GTD35 motors run on a 12V supply and include integrated drivers, simplifying the circuit design precise motor control during operation.
column43: The main microcontroller used in our robot is the ARM STM32F407VET6, chosen for its high processing speed, efficiency, and large number of I/O pins. Additionally, we use two extra STM32 units dedicated to sensors processing, each handling TSSP and line sensors separately to improve performance and reduce latency. For communication between robots, we use an ESP-32 Wroom module, selected for its reliable Wi-Fi capability and fast, low-noise data transmission between systems.
column44: The processor controls the motors using RS485 Protocol to send data to the driver motors and move the motors.
column45: Our robot uses TSSP sensors to detect the ball by identifying modulated infrared signals emitted at a specific frequency. The sensors are tuned to this frequency, allowing them to filter out ambient light and noise. By reading signal strength and direction from multiple sensors, the system determines the ball’s position relative to the robot, enabling accurate tracking and response during gameplay.
column46: Our line detection circuit uses phototransistor sensors to measure reflectivity differences between the green field and white lines. Each surface produces a different output value. We calculate a threshold, and if the reading is above it, it is green; if below, it is detected as a white line.
column47: Our robot uses ultrasonic sensors for navigation, with two on the sides and one at the back to detect obstacles and measure distance from field boundaries. These sensors are connected to the STM32, which processes their data. For positioning and orientation, we use the TD-Axis system, which provides directional data relative to the goal, allowing the robot to adjust its heading.
column48: The kicker system is run with just one pin in one direction allowing use maximum of power kicking force. It operates on a 12V power supply, with current ranging from 0.5A to 2A depending on load. The driver regulates power to the mechanism, ensuring consistent and reliable kicking performance.
column49: The dribbler system uses a DJI2212 920KV motor to rotate the front roller and control the ball. It is driven by a 30A motor driver connected to the power supply. The driver is controlled using PWM signals, allowing precise speed adjustment for stable, consistent, and efficient ball handling.
column52: One of our main electronics innovations is the use of three STM32 microcontrollers, allowing distributed processing for improved performance and reduced latency, especially for sensor handling. We also integrated an ESP-32 Wroom module for fast and reliable wireless communication between robots. Additionally, we designed a professional custom PCB, optimizing layout, power distribution, and signal integrity, which improved reliability, reduced noise, and made the overall system more compact and efficient.
column54: We use 36 TSSP58038 sensors arranged around the robot to detect the ball by receiving its modulated infrared signal at a specific frequency. The sensors are scanned in a timed cycle of 833 microseconds (1.2 kHz). When a sensor output goes low, it indicates detection of the ball signal. By analyzing active sensors, the system determines the ball direction and relative position for tracking.
column55: Our algorithm uses TSSP sensors to detect the ball’s direction and distance. The attacking robot moves toward the ball and captures it using the dribbler. The defensive robot aligns itself between the ball and our goal to block shots. When control changes, robots switch roles dynamically. This coordination is enabled through ESP32 communication, allowing real-time data sharing and TeamWork.
column56: We use three SRF05 ultrasonic sensors on the left, right, and back to measure distances from field walls. Data is sent to the STM32, which estimates position relative to boundaries. By combining this with orientation data, the robot adjusts motor outputs to maintain position and navigate accurately.
column57: We use phototransistor sensors arranged in cross and circular patterns to detect field lines. These sensors sense changes in surface reflectivity when crossing white boundaries. They are divided into four regions (front, back, left, right). When a region detects a line, the algorithm identifies its direction and moves the robot away to stay in bounds.
column58: Our scoring algorithm uses positional data to determine when the robot is close to the goal area. As the robot approaches the walls, it increases confidence that the ball is aligned with the goal opening. The dribbler maintains control of the ball while positioning, and once optimal alignment is detected, the kicker is activated to shoot the ball accurately toward the goal.
column59: We use sensor-based algorithms to continuously track the ball’s position and movement. When the sensors detect that the ball is moving toward our goal, the robot quickly adjusts its position to intercept it. The defensive robot places itself between the ball and the goal, using controlled movement to block or clear the ball, preventing the opponent from scoring.
column60: Yes, our robots communicate using ESP-32 modules. Each robot shares its distance to the ball with the other. Based on this data, they dynamically adjust their positions on the field. For example, the robot closer to the ball takes the attacking role, while the other switches to defense, improving teamwork and efficiency.
column61: One of our key algorithms is robot coordination and goal alignment. Using ESP-32 communication, robots share ball data and switch roles between attack and defense based on position. We also implemented vision-based goal detection, allowing the robot to automatically adjust its angle toward the goal. This improves teamwork, positioning, and shooting accuracy, increasing our chances of scoring.

The solenoid was positioned so as not to interfere with the motor.
The design optimizes the stroke and takes the camera's field of view into account.
