column41:
It uses an 11.1V battery, and the motor is powered directly by 11.1V.
The solenoid is powered by a voltage that has been boosted to 30V via a boost circuit, while the main microcontroller is powered by a voltage that has been stepped down to 5V.
column42:
The motor driver that drives the motor is not the main microcontroller.
A sub-microcontroller connected to the main microcontroller via UART drives the motor driver.
column43:
Teensy 4.1: Main microcontroller (integration and processing of all data)
Seeeduino XIAO RP2350*2: Motor drivers and line sensors (processing of individual tasks)
column44:
The Teensy 4.1 sends motor speed commands to the Seeeduino via UART.
The Seeeduino uses PWM to control each motor driver based on the received commands.
column45:
The system integrates and processes data from the four cameras mounted on the Robot.
The sensors used to determine the conditions for activating the kicker detect these conditions using LEDs and phototransistors.
column46:
Sensors are mounted on the underside of the robot.
Although there are only four sensors mounted on the robot, this is not a problem because when they detect an obstacle, the robot avoids it based on information from the camera.
column47: To estimate its position, the system uses ultrasonic sensors installed in all four directions and the position of the goal obtained from camera images. We use the BNO055 to determine direction.
column48:
When the microcontroller detects that the ball has entered the robot's intake port, it sends a current from the microcontroller to the FET.
The solenoid directly pushes the plate forward to kick the ball out.
column49: In the driver, the microcontroller sends an analog signal directly to the FET. From there, it controls the 11V current to power the driver.
column52:
The analog multiplexer supports only up to 16 pins. We optimized the placement of the 16 sensors capable of detecting lines to achieve the most effective configuration.
We solved this by arranging 32 phototransistors throughout the system and processing them in pairs.
column54: Our robots tracking the ball color with a UnitV camera for ball recognition and sending the results to the Teensy using UART.
column55: We use PD control for ball tracking. Variable gain allows quick turning at wide angles and suppresses oscillation near the front. The D-term applies only during convergence to prevent overshoot. Control parameters were optimized in a simulator under extreme high-speed conditions by visualizing approach trajectories and robot disturbances.
column56: The robot is equipped with ultrasonic distance sensors in four directions, which it uses to move toward the center of the court if the ball cannot be found, by utilizing the distance from the court walls.
column57: Sixteen line sensors feed a sub-MCU that reports only sensors detecting white. Each sensor has a known mounting angle; when several fire, we take the vector average of their angles to find the boundary, then add 180° to set an escape vector away from it. If the ball is near, we blend 70% escape with 30% pursuit. Ultrasonics prevent escaping into a wall, choosing the freest direction near corners.
column58: We have kicker and dribbler modes. In kicker mode we chase, face the target goal, and kick once the ball sensor confirms possession; a chase-angle calc guides the ball into the intake. Aiming uses the compass far out and camera goal-edges when close (hysteresis switch). kick() aligns, briefly dribbles, then fires ~30ms. In dribbler mode we secure the ball, turn away, advance, face goal, and shoot.
column59: In the Vision league, robots are characterized by their smaller size compared to those in the Infrared league. Because of this, the effectiveness of defense is lower than in the Infrared league, so we have assigned both robots to offense.
column60: Our robots does not have communication capabilities.
column61: One of our robots estimates distance using machine learning. The model takes camera-detected object coordinates as input and estimates the distance to items like the ball or goal. We used a distillation approach: a neural network trained on 200+ training and 80+ validation data points, whose outputs then trained a polynomial regression model. This model runs inference on a Teensy 4.1 in about 20μs per iteration, with accuracy held to a mean absolute error under 1cm.

column32: First-year team, so everything was designed from scratch in Onshape, driven by the 2026 rules (1500 g, 22 cm, 1.5 cm capture zone, 42 mm ball). High-stress parts are printed in Loctite 3172/Dura 56 resin on a Stratasys P3 DLP; low-stress parts in PLA on FDM; base plates laser-cut from 2 mm 6061 aluminum. Our key part is the dribbler: an RS-360 motor spins a 40A silicone hourglass bar at ~12,000 RPM that grips and centers the ball, bounded by the 1.5 cm capture zone.
column33: We built the robot using in-house and sponsor manufacturing. PLA parts were printed on our Bambu Lab P1S/P2S printers, and we cast the dribbler silicone. Stratasys printed resin parts, while NTNTech cut the 2 mm 6061 aluminum plates.
column34: We use 5 motors: four JGB37-520B gearmotors with encoders for a four-wheel omnidirectional X-drive, and one RS-360 motor for the dribbler. We chose four drive motors because soccer requires stable holonomic movement, allowing the robot to move toward the ball while rotating toward the goal. The RS-360 powers the dribbler independently, maintaining speeds of about 12,000 RPM.
column36: The dribbler is powered by an RS360 motor through a GT2 belt spinning at 12000 RPM An hourglasshaped Shore 40A silicone roller cast in-house grips and centers the ball The axle floats on rubber band tension for impact absorption while a concave 3D printed seat below provides a second centering point
column38: Our main mechanical innovation is the floating dribbler system. An RS-360 motor drives an hourglass-shaped Shore 40A silicone roller through a GT2 belt. The roller continuously centers the ball, while a floating axle suspended by rubber-band tension absorbs impacts and maintains contact. A concave 3D-printed seat beneath the ball provides a second centering point. This creates a stable, self-aligning ball control system that improves possession and handling during collisions.
column41: Our robot uses a 3 cell 18650 lithium battery 12.8V. The battery powers the motors and dribbler directly, while a buck converter provides 5V for the Teensy, ESP32-S3 cameras, and IMU. A 3.3V regulator powers the sensors. Custom PCBs distribute power and connect all systems.
column42: We use four DC gearmotors in an omnidirectional X-drive. Two L298N dual H-bridge drivers control the motors, while a Teensy 4.1 provides PWM speed control and direction signals. Each wheel is driven independently, allowing the robot to move and rotate freely in any direction. (283 characters)
column43: We use a Teensy 4.1 as the main controller because of its fast 600 MHz processor and ability to handle many sensors simultaneously. It manages ball and line detection, navigation, motor and dribbler control, robot strategy, and communication. Two ESP32-S3 modules, each connected to a camera facing a different direction, provide 240° vision. Each ESP32 processes images locally and sends object data to the Teensy via UART, enabling fast vision without overloading the main processor.
column44: The Teensy continuously reads sensor data, calculates the robot and ball positions, determines the desired movement and rotation, converts them into wheel speeds using omnidirectional kinematics, and sends PWM commands to the motor drivers. This enables smooth and responsive movement.
column45: The robot detects the ball using 20 TSSP4038 infrared sensors arranged around its perimeter. The Teensy scans the sensors and calculates the ball's direction based on the strongest signals. Two ESP32-S3 camera modules, one facing forward and one backward, provide a combined 240° field of view. The cameras detect goals and field landmarks, while each ESP32 processes images locally and sends only relevant data to the Teensy, reducing processing load and improving navigation.
column46: We use four TCS34725 color sensors to detect the white field lines. A TCA9548A I²C multiplexer allows multiple sensors with the same address to operate on one bus. The Teensy reads each sensor and uses its position to determine where the line is and respond appropriately.
column47: We use a BNO055 IMU for heading, angular velocity, and motion tracking, helping the robot maintain its orientation while moving. Two ESP32-S3 cameras (front and rear) detect goals and field features for localization. Line sensors provide additional positional information and help keep the robot inside the field.
column48: we dont have a kicker
column49: The dribbler uses an RS-360 motor controlled by a PWM-driven MOSFET circuit. Power is transferred through a GT2 timing belt to an hourglass-shaped silicone roller that grips and centers the ball. A floating axle with rubber-band suspension maintains contact, improving ball control and possession.
column52: One electronics innovation we are most proud of is our modular PCB design. We use dedicated board-to-board connectors between the upper, middle, and lower PCBs, allowing the robot to be assembled, serviced, and repaired quickly. This reduces wiring complexity, improves reliability, and makes replacing damaged components much easier during competitions. The modular design also simplifies future upgrades without requiring a complete redesign of the electronics.
column54: Our robot detects the ball with a 360° IR ring: 19 sensors around the robot plus one pocket sensor in the dribbler. Each sensor is sampled quickly, and LOW readings mean the IR ball signal was detected. We count these hits as strength, use a weighted vector sum to calculate the ball angle, and use the pocket sensor to know when the ball is captured
column55: The IR ring gives the ball angle and strength. If the ball is far, the robot drives fast toward it; when it is close, it slows down, centers the ball in front of the dribbler, turns the dribbler on, and pulls it into the pocket. Both robots use the same code, but the one with the better ball reading attacks, while the other moves to support/rebound.
column56: We do not calculate exact X/Y position. Instead, we use relative field awareness. The compass keeps our heading stable, the cameras detect the goals and field features, and the color sensors detect white boundary lines. The robot uses this information to know if it is facing the correct goal, if it is near the edge, and which direction to drive using omni-wheel movement.
column57: Our robot detects the field lines with four bottom color sensors and also with the cameras. The color sensors identify white by checking high brightness and similar RGB values. If a line is detected, line avoidance becomes the highest priority. The robot immediately drives away from the line, turns back toward the field, enters a short recovery state, and only then returns to chasing the ball.
column58: To score, the robot first captures the ball with the dribbler and uses the pocket IR sensor to confirm control. Then the cameras find the target goal and the robot aligns toward it. The dribbler keeps the ball stable until release. We mainly use a push-shot/spinback shot: the robot spins or drives forward, releases the dribbler, and pushes the ball toward the goal.
column59: We do not use a fixed goalie. Both robots are active attackers, but they communicate and choose roles. The robot with the better ball reading pressures and captures the ball, while the second robot moves to a support/rebound position. This helps block attacks early, reduce opponent control, and cover more of the field without the robots colliding.
column60: Yes. The robots communicate with each other through the ESP32 system and share ball visibility, ball angle, ball strength, if they have the ball, and role data. This helps them decide which robot attacks and which robot supports/rebounds, reducing collisions and covering more of the field.
column61: Our software innovation combines a pre-match testing app, a clear state machine, and a spinback shot. The app is only for testing before the match: telemetry, sensors, motors, dribbler, camera calibration, and goal color lock. During the match it has no control. The state machine organizes behaviors, and the spinback shot holds the ball, spins, then releases it toward the goal.

column32: Mechanical design used Autodesk Fusion, Desmos, and Bambu Studio for modelling, mirror optimisation, and 3D printing. Three prototypes iterated on functionality of primary components, arrangement of key components, and ease of manufacturing and debugging. Key improvements included optimising the robot's vision by designing a custom CNC mirror, implementing a tube instead of standoffs for camera vision, and swapping nuts for standoffs on motor mounts to speed up assembly and part replacement.
column33: A Bambu Lab P1S 3D printer, Carvera CNC machine were used to build the design. JLCPCB manufactured PCBs designed in Autodesk Fusion, with components hand-soldered in-lab. Motor mounts and other 3D printed components were reprinted due to tolerances.
column34: Four motors spaced at 90-degree intervals were used to balance force output in all directions and minimise axial rotation. Three motors provided insufficient force; five exceeded size constraints. Omni-wheels were designed in Autodesk Fusion, each housing 15 rollers secured between two PLA plates. Designing our own wheels allowed spare parts to be printed without having to ensure compatibility.
column35: A Tahaka CB1037 push-solenoid was used within our kicker assembly. We designed the capture zone of the robot around this solenoid such that the solenoid was screwed onto two walls on either side of the capture zone. However, we were not able to develop the kicker by Internationals.
column36: A two-piece semi-hyperbolic roller dribbler was designed with a Maxon DCX 19 motor at a 1:2.833 gear ratio for speed. The roller was split into two sections to negate differential surface speeds and lateral drift from varying radii. The dribbler was not completed in time for Internationals.
column38: Physics and math principles alongside Desmos' regression function were used to derive a continuous tangent function for the mirror curve using angle of incidence/reflection and differentiation. Anti-differentiation yielded the final curve expression, with points exported at 0.01 mm increments matching the Carvera CNC's accuracy into Fusion as an SVG. The mirror was polished with liquid sandpaper and WD40. This eliminated all field blind spots, increasing the robot's competitive edge.
column41: Powered by a 3S (12.6V) battery, the circuit includes a smoothing capacitor, reverse-polarity MOSFET protection, a main switch, logic/motor fuses and switches, and 5V/3.3V regulators. To maintain voltage above 11.5V, the Teensy is connected to a voltage divider, removing external indicator use.
column42: Four DC motors are driven by individual VNH7070AS controllers, receiving INA, INB and PWM signals from the Teensy. Early latch-up issues caused by back-powering were resolved by adding pull-down resistors, software switches, and initialising controllers in brake mode before enabling PWM.
column43: We chose a Teensy 4.1 microcontroller for our robot. It has 55 digital pins and 18 analog pins, allowing us to connect and control all the components on our PCBs. It also supports a range of serial communication protocols, such as I2C and UART. Furthermore, it has a 600 MHz processor which allows for ultra-fast processing of complex logic such as line avoidance.
column44: Each motor receives two digital 'direction' signals and one PWM 'speed' signal from the Teensy. Individual motor speeds and directions are calculated using the scalar projection of each motor's axis onto the robot's desired direction of motion, allowing smooth and accurate omnidirectional movement.
column45: Using Python in OpenMV IDE, our camera tracks balls/goals, sending blob data to a Teensy via UART. To fix 2025 Nationals lighting issues, we tightened LAB thresholds to minimize ranges and raised blob size limits to filter noise like hands. We added hardware indents to hide reflective hot glue, and a software ROI circle restricted detection to the circular mirror, eliminating outside interference.
column46: The line detection circuit uses 48 LED/phototransistor modules (16 outer, 32 inner) read via three 16-channel multiplexers to save Teensy pins. A PWM-controlled N-channel MOSFET regulates LED brightness for optimal contrast. We used an oscilloscope to debug the MUX signals (see Short Video TDP).
column47: We find blob angles and distances from the OpenMV (UART). We find robot heading with a BNO055; to stop IMU resets from motor current spikes, we added separate motor and logic GND planes. We upgraded from the OpenMV H7+ to the OpenMV N6 (90 vs 50 FPS).
column48: Unfortunately whilst we were unable to integrate a kicker system in time for Internationals, for the first and second prototypes of the robot we developed a kicker PCB which involved discharging 50V through a 10,000 μF capacitor into a Tahaka CB1037 push-solenoid.
column49: Unfortunately whilst we were unable to integrate a dribbler system in time for Internationals, for the first and second prototypes of the robot we added an extra JST connector onto our main PCB to allow a Maxon DCX 19 motor to be connected.
column52:
Our greatest source of pride is the dual-ring light sensor PCB with PWM brightness control. The board features two concentric rings: an outer ring of 16 modules for early line detection and a full inner ring of 32 modules for precise line angle calculation. Each module pairs an LED and phototransistor — when passing over the white line, reflected light increases, spiking the phototransistor's output.
To read all 48 sensors efficiently, three 16-channel multiplexers reduce the pin requirement to just 4 digital and 3 analog pins on the Teensy 4.1, connected via a single FFC cable.
Our key innovation is MOSFET-based PWM brightness control. Previously, fixed LED brightness caused contrast issues — too bright saturated the phototransistors; too dim lost the line entirely. We added an AO3404 N-channel MOSFET with its gate driven by a Teensy PWM pin, source to GND, and drain to the LED cathodes. This lets us tune brightness in software instantly, maximising contrast between the dark field and white line under any venue lighting.
During debugging, cold solder joints on the FFC connectors caused two multiplexer select signals to fail. Oscilloscope probing confirmed the Teensy outputs were correct, isolating the fault to the joints. Reflowing with flux resolved the issue.
Finally, our line angle algorithm was upgraded from vector averaging — which reacted too slowly at the boundary — to a cluster-based approach, producing sharper angle changes when the robot exits the field.
column54: We use an OpenMV N6 with VGA resolution cropped to 400x400 pixels to track blobs through LAB thresholds, finding their x- and y-coordinates. Because they range from 0-400, we send each value each over two bytes through UART to the Teensy, with two start bytes (255) to ensure correct data when read from the Teensy.
column55: Our attacker orbit algorithm uses a ball angle offset and a distance scale function to calculate movement angle and speed. Once the ball is in the capture zone, the robot surges towards the goal, pushing the ball.
column56: We are yet to fully implement a localisation system. However, if we did, we would use our camera to find the goal angles and distances to estimate robot position. For increased accuracy, we are looking to add a LRF PCB on top of the mirror mount in the future for accurate wall distances. We could then make a coordinate system on the field for more accurate and smooth movement.
column57: In setup, we read all phototransistors and add a set threshold to find each sensor’s white threshold. In game, sensors above the white thresholds are grouped into clusters, whose midpoint angles are used to calculate the line angle. The outer ring gives early detection, while the inner ring is prioritised due to its circular shape increasing line angle accuracy (see GitHub repository).
column58: On our final robot design, we didn't account for a kicker nor a dribbler due to time constraints. However, if we had both, we would consider using the dribbler to control and hide the ball from the opposition defender. Additionally, we would test kicking whenever the goal area is larger than a certain threshold. Currently, we rely on momentum and the attacker orbit algorithm to score goals.
column59: Our defender stays on the line and moves along the line to be positioned in between the ball and the goal. The movement values are calculated using horizontal and vertical PIDs, where the horizontal PID takes the input of the ball angle and the setpoint of 0, and the vertical PID uses the line position and goal distance. If the ball is too close, the defender will surge towards the ball.
column60: We have not yet implemented Bluetooth-based switching, but the main PCB is designed to support a Bluetooth module. Our current plan is to implement a simple switching case where the attacker and the defender swap roles whenever the ball enters the defender’s capture zone. This tactic would enable the defender to quickly score when the opposition attacker is out of position.
column61: The largest software innovation is line avoidance. We use a double ring layout to provide both early line detection and accurate line angle readings; the partial outer ring was ignored if the inner ring saw the line. We also calculate line width by finding the perpendicular distance from the robot to the line chord, allowing the robot to change its speed depending on how far it is over the line.

column32:
We used AutoCAD/CorelDRAW and Onshape/Fusion 360. The chassis was designed around our Maxon motors for a low center of gravity and 3D-printed in ASA for impact and heat resistance.
Initially, the robot exceeded the 1.5kg limit and flexed under stress. We resolved this by cutting weight-saving pockets and adding reinforcing ribs in CAD. For printing, we optimized slicer settings with thicker outer walls, lower infill, and adjusted print orientation to maximize structural strength.
column33: We 3D-printed our ASA chassis. We outsourced PCBs, CNC couplings, and sheet metal brackets to JLCPCB. To resolve ASA thermal shrinkage during assembly, we adjusted CAD offsets and hole clearances for a perfect fit.
column34: Our 4-motor holonomic drive uses Maxon RE16 DC motors for rapid, omnidirectional X/Y translation and rotation. For optimal RoboCup carpet grip, we engineered custom omni-wheels featuring a rigid stainless steel hub (lowering the CG) and 17 sub-rollers fitted with nitrile rubber bands. This ensures high traction and durability during aggressive maneuvers. Full specs are in our BOM.
column35: We engineered a kicker using an EML-1039B solenoid and a custom Fusion 360 striking plate. It features an ultra-low clearance for the new standardized ball. The wide plate geometry maximizes contact area, ensuring a highly stable, powerful transfer of kinetic energy.
column36: The system is driven by a high-torque T-Motor V2306 V2 (KV2400) paired with a 30A ESC. It features a split-mechanism design that utilizes lateral tension springs to generate dynamic, constant downward pressure, effectively locking the ball against the chassis during aggressive rotations.
column38: Our key mechanical innovation is the active dribbler system. Initially, we utilized a static-height mount, but testing showed the ball easily escaped during rapid movement. Realizing the need for dynamic downward pressure, we engineered a spring-loaded, split-assembly design. A fixed base connects to a pivoting upper unit via springs. This mechanism actively presses downward, creating a continuous reactive force that securely locks the ball in place during aggressive maneuvers.
column41: Our JLCPCB mainboard splits a 3S 2200mAh 30C LiPo into 3 isolated domains: raw 12.6V for Maxon motors, a 3.3V/5A buck for Teensy 4.1 logic, and an isolated 48V boost for the EML-1040B kicker. This prevents brownouts and strictly meets RCJ 2026 safety limits.
column42: To drive four Maxon RE16s, we use two WAYNESTARK V3.0 modules powered by 12.6V. A Teensy 4.1 sends 3.3V PWM signals for precise H-bridge speed control. These robust modules provide extreme thermal headroom and safely isolate sensitive logic from motor back-EMF spikes.
column43: Our 3-MCU distributed architecture ensures parallel execution without bottlenecks. A Teensy 4.0 processes heavy camera/IR data into directional vectors. A compact ESP32-S3 handles line detection, ensuring zero-latency boundary braking. Finally, a 600MHz Teensy 4.1 acts as the central hub. Freed from sensor polling, its abundant I/O handles high-level game logic, holonomic kinematics, and precise PWM control for our four Maxon motors.
column44: The Teensy 4.1 uses inverse kinematics to translate X/Y/Theta vectors into motor speeds. A closed-loop PID reads encoders to dynamically correct speed errors. Corrected 3.3V PWM signals are then amplified by WAYNESTARK V3.0 drivers to regulate 12.6V motor power for precise holonomic movement.
column45: Our dual-sensor system combines IR and vision. 12 radial TSOP4038 receivers detect IR signals, with a low-pass filter converting pulses to analog voltages. An ESP32-S3 multiplexes this data to a Teensy 4.0 via UART. Simultaneously, four MaixCam Lites (GC4653, 145° lenses) send visual data over UART. The Teensy 4.0 aggregates both streams, forwarding integrated results to the main controller for decision-making.
column46: Our custom Line Sensor PCB detects white lines using 30 NJL7502 sensors and 30 white LEDs spaced at 12mm. Two 16-channel multiplexers optimize pin usage. A Xiao ESP32-S3 processes this data and transmits computed line positions to the Teensy 4.1 via UART.
column47: The Teensy 4.1 reads four analog ultrasonic sensors via ADC for obstacle avoidance. It also uses a BNO055 IMU via I2C for precise heading control. For localization, four MaixCam Lite cameras detect the goal's angle and distance, allowing the robot to estimate its relative field position.
column48: To maximize kick power, we use a 12V 35N electromagnet powered by a 48V/2A boost module and two 2.2mF capacitors. We solved inrush current shutdowns by adding a 47Ω resistor. A Teensy 4.1 uses dual optocouplers to safely control P-channel and N-channel MOSFETs with a flyback diode.
column49: To fix low startup torque, we upgraded the dribbler to a T-Motor V2306 (KV2400) and Hobbywing 30A ESC. To improve ball retention, we replaced the static-height mount with a spring-loaded pivot mechanism. This applies dynamic downward pressure to the silicone roller, actively securing the ball.
column52: The electronic subsystem we are most proud of is our custom-engineered Top-Level Sensor Aggregation PCB, designed to provide flawless 360-degree environmental awareness while completely eliminating computational bottlenecks. To achieve comprehensive visual tracking without blind spots, we engineered a quad-camera array utilizing four Maix Cam Lite AI vision modules mounted strategically on the upper board. Processing four concurrent, high-framerate video streams would paralyze a standard microcontroller, so we routed these cameras into a dedicated Teensy 4.0. This processor acts purely as a vision aggregator, synthesizing the four discrete fields of view into unified, prioritized targeting vectors. Complementing this vision system is our proprietary infrared tracking ring, which consists of 12 TSOP4038 IR receivers uniformly distributed around the PCB's perimeter. The critical hardware innovation in this circuit was the integration of custom Low-Pass Filters (LPF) on the sensor output lines. In chaotic tournament environments, high-frequency electrical noise and erratic ambient light interference frequently cause false-positive sensor triggers. By implementing these analog RC filters, we successfully smoothed the signal envelopes, delivering pristine, noise-free data to a dedicated Seeeduino XIAO ESP32-S3 coprocessor. The ESP32-S3 rapidly polls all 12 sensors and runs a continuous trigonometric algorithm to calculate the precise angle and relative distance of the infrared ball. Finally, both the Teensy 4.0 and the ESP32-S3 act as localized sensory brains, streaming only their finalized, highly accurate coordinate data down to the main Teensy 4.1 motor controller via high-speed serial. This advanced distributed architecture guarantees that our robot's main logic loop receives instantaneous, deeply filtered telemetry without ever having to pause motor execution to process raw sensor data.
column54: Our system fuses IR and camera data (Teensy 4.0 & ESP32-S3). For near-field, an EMA filter and Top-5 Vector Synthesis process IR signals, yielding a 1-degree trajectory. For far-field, cameras track the ball, goal, and lines for absolute spatial localization. Fusing these streams pinpoints the ball's global coordinates, enabling optimized navigation and responsive control.
column55: Our attacker uses a blended chase algorithm to smoothly wrap around the ball, avoiding collisions. It combines a tangent calculation for orbiting at wide angles and a PD controller for precise, overshoot-free frontal interception. A dynamic factor seamlessly shifts between these paths based on alignment, while pursuit speed dynamically scales up to 1.5x based on distance.
column56: Our sensor fusion integrates camera and ultrasonic data for precise (x,y) field localization. To refine pose estimation, a BNO055 IMU provides real-time yaw compensation, mitigating rotational drift during aggressive turns. This highly stable spatial awareness drives our high-level control logic, enabling the robot to partition tactical zones and execute position-dependent strategies.
column57: For robust boundary retention, our vector-based line avoidance algorithm uses a radial sensor array. Binarized analog telemetry detects the white line. Detections map to geometric coordinates and fuse via vector superposition, yielding a resultant vector for proximity. Contiguous triggers form a stable normal vector; fragmented detections use spatial clustering for a reliable escape trajectory.
column58: Our state machine drives offense. Without the ball, the robot centers. Upon detection, it intercepts and engages the active dribbler. In the defensive half, it shields the ball with its chassis and navigates the outer edge to bypass center traffic. In the offensive half, it pivots, uses vision to target the largest unguarded gap, and fires the 48V EML-1040B kicker for a precise shot.
column59: Our defensive state machine fuses ultrasonic and vision data for precise localization, shifting between central fallback, PID center defense, and lateral tracking. To counter impacts, it monitors absolute heading for immediate PID yaw corrections. For boundary retention, the NJL7502L array calculates a repulsive normal vector to keep the robot in the goal zone and prevent out-of-bounds penalties.
column60: While inter-robot communication is advantageous, our current models operate independently. Due to limited testing time this season, we prioritized core mechanical reliability and single-agent algorithms. Looking ahead, we plan to implement Radio Frequency (RF) modules for robot communication. This will enable dynamic role assignment, prevent double-targeting, and execute collaborative plays.
column61:
Our ChaseBall Algorithm resolves overshooting via four elements:
PD Control: Angle error drives Kp alignment and Kd damping for stable interceptions.
Tangent Path: Calculates a curved orbital approach to avoid collisions.
Blending: Shifts between PD (narrow angles) and Tangent (wide angles) based on the ball's angle.
Speed: Scales up to 1.5x for distance, slowing for close-quarter control.
This dynamic fusion ensures faster acquisition and precise offensive positioning.

column11: We worked every Tuesday for 2 hours and would work all day on Saturdays as needed
column12: We worked at our school and at our homes
column14: Croatian open 2026 – infrared league, Austrian open 2026 – infrared league, European championship 2026 – infrared league
column15: Our mentor helped us find sponsorships and provided all the parts for our robots, he helped us organise and schedule meetings to work on the robot.
column16: We communicated through a WhatsApp group
column17: We used ChatGPT to translate documentation to English and we used Calude for programming
column32: We opted for a closed octagonal chassis design. Our goal was to make the chassis as compact and possible and keep the assembly of the robot easy and quick. We made the chassis to consist of as few parts as possible in order to achieve as compact of a design as possible. Components like motors, motor drivers and the kicker are all placed on the bottom part of the chassis and a designated placed in the chassis for a battery was also added.
column33: All chassis parts were 3D printed at our school and PCBs were ordered from JLCPCB. Parts such as screws and nuts were bought locally, and custom polyurethane wheel treads were ordered locally from Gumiimpex.
column34: Each robot has four Joinmax JMP-BE 3561 motors since they provide sufficient power and speed for the needs of our robots. We also designed custom wheels and opted on a dual-layer design, where each wheel consists of two basic wheels in order to reduce the gap between two rubber rollers for it to achieve better grip.
column35: As our kicker we use a powerful solenoid motor and have designed a custom PCB to control it. Two main parts of the PCB are a strong MOSFET and a level shifter which is needed to be able to trigger the kicker reliably with the signal from Teensy.
column38: Some of the most valuable upgrades were making dual-layer wheels which provided us whit better movement control and better grip, reducing sliding during sudden direction changes, and addition of a kicker which allowed us to score goals even if the robot is far from the opposing goal.
column41: Our robot operates on three voltage levels: 12V, 5V, and 3.3V. Battery is connected to our power supply PCB which provides 12V for our motor drivers and the kicker, the PCB also has two step-down voltage regulators that provide 3.3V and 5V for parts of the robot such as microcontrollers and sensors.
column42: For our motor drive circuit, we use four ML-R 1x50A MRMS motor drivers which are powered by 12V from our power supply PCB. Each motor has its own motor driver and is controlled using two pins connected to the micro controller; a PWM pin to manage the speed and a DIR pin to manage motor’s direction.
column43: We use Teensy 4.1 as our main micro controller because of its processing power and sufficient number of I/O pins as well as for its proven reliability. Teensy controls the motors and all of our robots’ sensors except the ball sensor. As our secondary micro controller, we picked XIAO ESP32-C6 since it doesn’t take up as much space while still having enough pins to connect our custom ball sensor to it, and it allows our robots to be able to communicate using the ESP now protocol.
column44: We have a function that receives the desired speed and movement angel. using trigonometric functions movement speed and direction are calculated for each motor. the micro controller outputs the wanted speed and direction to each of the motors using PWM pin for speed and DIR pin for direction.
column45: We made a custom ball sensor as a part of our main PCB that consists of 16 IR receivers aligned in a circle, they are connected to a 16-channel multiplexer which is connected to our secondary micro controller XIAO ESP-C6. Our secondary micro controller calculates the ball angle and that data is sent to our main micro controller Teensy 4.1 via UART. We also use an openMV H7+ camera facing forward so that when the ball is detected in front of the robot we are able to get robot's distance from it.
column46: We made a custom line detection sensor which consists of 48 ALS-PT19-315C/L177/TR8 ambient light sensors each paired with a white LED to help in harsh light conditions. the 48 sensors are connected to three 16-channel analog multiplexers which are connected to our main micro controller.
column47: We use four ultrasonic distance sensors to roughly estimate the robots’ position and know how far it is from the field boundaries which helps us to make a more reliable line avoidance function. Each of the four sensors is connected to our main micro controller via a single analog pin. For maintaining the direction the robot faces we use BNO055 IMU which is connected to Teensy via I2C.
column48: the kicker PCB receives a 3.3V signal from Teensy. the signal goes to a level shifter which outputs a 5V signal which is needed to activate the MOSFET. When activated, the MOSFET connects the kicker to 12V and triggers it. there is also a strong capacitor to allow for more powerful kicks.
column52: We are the most proud of our custom ball sensor since it has proven to be much more precise than commercially available ones and it allowed us to make better ball following functions. We are also very proud of our kicker PCB since it has been quite difficult making a reliable board that is able to withstand high current draw when the kicker is activated.
column54: Our secondary micro controller reads all 16 channels of a multiplexer to see which sensors are detecting the ball. The ball angle is then calculated using a weighted circular average of all the sensors that saw the ball. Calibration and smoothing (EMA) is than applied to the raw angle to reduce noise and the final angle is than sent to the main micro controller via UART.
column55: We made a few different balls following functions for our attacker robot. The function we use the most is made so that the robot is always trying to get directly behind the ball to trigger the kicker system. Another function we use makes the robot overshoot the ball slightly on purpose in order to be able to score a goal from neutral points near the opposing goal.
column56: we use ultrasonic distance sensors to determine robots' distance from the white lines by detecting where the walls of the filed are. Using that info, we made a ball following function which slows the robot down as it is approaching the lines in order to ensure a reliable line detection. This way we are able to set the top movement speed much higher and not worry about line detection reliability.
column57: The robot uses our custom line sensor to detect the white lines. The sensors are divided into four groups (front, back, left, right) and when a line is detected the robot calculates the opposite direction from there the line was detected. to ensure reliable line detection the robot gradually slows down as it is approaching the white lines.
column58: We mainly rely on the kicker against teams with a goalkeeper; the robot always gets directly behind the ball and uses the camera to aim at the goal when the robot has the ball. Against teams without the goalkeeper, we use a program that is able to score goals when the ball is placed on neutral spots near the opposing goal since our robot is quite fast and often is the first to get to the ball.
column59: Our goalkeeper robot relies on a camera facing backwards to know the position and distance of its own goal as it is moving left or right to get in front of the ball. The robot always turns so that the back of it is always facing the goal center ensuring that it moves in a semicircle around the goal. To keep a desired distance from the goal a PID-inspired program is used to align the robot.
column60: Our robots are able to communicate using ESP now protocol. We tried implementing this functionality to our program but unfortunately, we weren’t able to find a useful function for it and decided that the robots work better individually without communication.
column61: Software innovation we are the most proud of is our addition of using distance sensors to determine the distance from the walls of the field and slowing down as the robot is approaching the white lines. By using this program, we noticed a rise in the average amount of goals the robot scores during a match and a drop in the number of times the robot goes out to bounds because of the more reliable line detection due to the robot slowing down.
column64: both robots cost 1,444.66€, we spend about 500€ on failed versions of PCBs and fried components. the environment was provided to us by the school for free.
column65: robots were fully funded by our school. our travel to competitions and other expenses while at competitions are funded by sponsorships.

column16:
We have a Discord server which we use to communicate with each other. During each meeting, we track our 
progress by setting new tasks and marking already completed ones in a spreadsheet.
column17:
We used ChatGPT as a learning and support tool for electronics. We also used GitHub Copilot to speed up coding, writing and completing
functions.
column32:
We used Fusion 360 to make our design and Eagle for our PCBs.
Firstly, we created a 3D model of the part in Fusion 360. We have our whole robot designed 
there so it's pretty easy adding new things to our robot since the rest of it is already 
precisely modeled. Later we got them made from aluminum or we 3D printed them 
ourselves.  
There are countless hours and versions behind the way our robots look now from material 
selections to the actual parts placements.
column33:
To build our design, we began by 3D printing all parts of the chassis.
Plastic components were printed in-house using PET-G filament.
For the 360-degree mirror, we worked with a local company that used vacuum forming with 
laminated polystyrene.
column34:
We have five motors. 
Four motors are used for movement, these are the “Maxon EC45 flat 70W”.
We also made our own omni wheels, as we needed 6cm diameter ones and weren’t able to find any suitable on the market. 
Each omni wheel consists of two 3D-printed halves, a circular iron wire axle (1 mm thick), 
and 24 small idler rollers.
One motor is used as dribbler.
column35:
Our kicker is a high current solenoid which we designed ourselves. To save space we also 
made it a flat solenoid as it’s commonly possible to see in the Small Size League (SSL).  
We then designed the plunger of the solenoid consisting of non-magnetic part 
and magnetic part which we got machined.
column36:
Then there are 2 options for a point mounted dribblers high mounted and bottom mounted.
The bottom mounted were found superior since they didn't push out the ball before 
getting into the dribbling position.
column38:
We try not to overcomplicate our hardware since we prioritize consistent design over 
flashy ones. However, we had at least 7 cable breakages on our maxon motors last year 
since they are brushless with encoders and each has around 20 cables. We managed to 
create our own pcb that is directly soldered onto the motor and has board-to-board 
connectors coming from it ensuring a stable and rigid connection. Also we are really proud 
of our dribbler, we only made it wider to capture the ball easier.
column41:
Our power system uses two 3S LiPo batteries in series (22.8 V), which supplies power to our motor drivers and motors.
We use a 5 V, 5 A buck converter to 5 V for the Raspberry Pi 5. From there, we regulate the voltage to 3.3 V to power our STM32.
For the kicker, we employ a 47.5 V boost converter.
column42:
We drive our motors using Maxon ESCON 24/2 motor drivers. Each motor is equipped with an encoder that provides position feedback at a resolution of 1024 counts per revolution for accurate speed and position control. 
The ESCON motor drivers receive PWM control signals from our STM32.
column43: Our robot uses a Raspberry Pi 5 with a Hailo-8L AI accelerator and two 32-bit STM32 microcontrollers. The Raspberry Pi runs vision, communication, and high-level control, using its multi-core CPU for parallel processing. The STM32F767 handles time-critical tasks such as motor control, kicker, IMU, and user interface, while the STM32G474 processes line sensor and LiDAR data. We chose STM32 over Arduino for its higher performance, richer peripherals, and industrial relevance.
column44: The Raspberry Pi 5 calculates movement direction, speed, and rotation from sensor and sends it to the STM32, which generates PWM signals for four ESCON motor drivers with encoder feedback. The dribbler runs at a constant speed and stops briefly during kicks.
column45:
Our vision system uses two cameras, both connected to the Raspberry Pi 5. One camera 
faces forward and is used for precise ball detection in front of the robot. The second 
camera is angled upward toward an omnidirectional mirror.
We access the camera feeds using the Picamera 2 interface.
The front-facing camera stream is processed by the Hailo-8L AI.
The mirror camera is currently processed using traditional computer vision methods.
column46:
At the bottom of our robot, we have 8 IR sensors with IR LEDs next to them. LEDs light up 
the field and the IR sensors measure how much light reflects back. White lines reflect 
more light than the green field, so they will have a higher value.
column47:
We use the LD19 LiDAR to determine the robot’s position on the field. It provides a 2D point cloud that we process to detect the field walls and calculate our location.
The LiDAR is connected via UART to the bottom STM32 (STM32G474), which handles 
sensor preprocessing. The IMU is also connected over UART, but to the top STM32 
(STM32F767), which manages motion control and orientation tracking.
column48: Our kicker uses a 47.5 V boost converter to charge 4.4 mF capacitors. Charging current is limited with two high-power SMD resistors in parallel to prevent diode damage. A gate driver switches a MOSFET from a 15 V supply for a fast 28 A kick lasting 10 ms, protecting the solenoid and circuit.
column49: Our dribbler uses a high-RPM motor, typically running at 4,000 RPM, to drive a silicone-covered bar. The bottom-mounted design lets the mechanism move back, maintaining ball contact. We use A60 Shore silicone, cast in a 3D-printed mold around the dribbler rod for optimal grip.
column52:
We are really proud of our kicker since it allows us to shoot really powerful shots with the 
benefit of still being compact and not taking up a lot of space. When we compared our 
kick strength with other teams we found out that our solenoids volume was fraction of 
theirs while giving the same amount of power or more. Thanks to the plunger shape, the 
kicks are also precise, which was a huge issue we faced last year.
column54: The Raspberry Pi 5 captures images using Picamera2. The front camera uses a Hailo-8L with a custom neural network to detect the ball, while the mirror camera uses OpenCV color detection. From the ball's bounding box, we estimate distance using the pinhole camera model and calculate its angle from the image center. For the mirror camera, angle is obtained by linear interpolation.
column55: Our robots use atacker or goalkeeper mode. The attacker catches the ball in two ways: for balls near the sides or back, it rotates toward the ball and drives straight to it. For balls in the center, it stays facing the opponent’s goal and moves around the ball if needed before catching it. The goalkeeper only approaches the ball when it is very close, otherwise it remains in a defensive position.
column56:
We use a LiDAR sensor to determine our robot’s position on the field, it gives us distances ang angles. We then apply a Hough transform to detect the field 
walls and estimate the robot’s exact position.
Once the current position is known, we calculate the direction and distance to the target 
location, where our robot will go.
column57:
We use LiDAR and IR sensors to detect lines and obstacles. The LiDAR sensor allows us to 
detect walls or obstacles in advance, enabling the system to decelerate smoothly before 
getting too close – this helps prevent the robot from crossing the line too quickly, which 
could cause the IR sensors to miss it.
column58: After catching the ball, the attacker rotates toward the center of the opponent’s goal based on its field position and kicks. If it is near an opponent’s corner, it first moves slightly away until it has a clear shot. When catching the ball near a sideline, it dribbles along the line toward the opponent’s corner before turning and shooting.
column59: The goalkeeper stays between the ball (or opponent) and the goal using camera and LiDAR. If the ball is lost, it uses shared or tracked positions. LiDAR identifies opponents by excluding its teammate. When close to the ball, it attempts a kickoff; otherwise, it moves to the best blocking position, switching to a tighter defensive path when the ball is near the goal.
column60:
Our robots communicate with each other using Bluetooth. They share key information 
such as the position of the ball, their own current positions on the field, and use this data 
to coordinate roles, dynamically deciding which robot should act as the striker and which 
as the goalkeeper.
column61: Our opponent detection algorithm processes LiDAR data by removing known objects such as field walls, goals, and our own robots (including teammate). The remaining points are clustered and treated as opponent positions. This allows detection of enemy robots without vision. The goalkeeper uses it to track opponents even when the ball is not visible, while the attacker uses it for basic avoidance during movement and shooting.

column15:
Mechanical design. 
Our coach instructed us on how to use design software like SolidWorks and AutoCAD to design our robot.
column16: We communicated through a WhatsApp group to agree on the times and days when we would meet within the school to work, and we used a Google Drive to share modifications to designs and codes among ourselves.
column17: We are using AI to learn how to program our OpenMV cameras because this is the first year we have used them.
column32: We used SolidWorks design software, taking into account the materials we already had and those we planned to acquire, to create a design that would allow us to manipulate the ball and easily direct it towards the goal. We had to make some modifications and adjustments to ensure our design had enough space for all the parts, such as motors, sensors, dribbler, kicker, wheels, PCBs, etc.
column33: Our structure was made in our workshop using 3D printing and various screws. The PCBs were created with Easy EDA software, etched using a laser and acid etching machine, and subsequently processed with JLCPCB.
column34: We use 4 motors for the movement of each robot, in addition to 4 motors for the dribbler system. We use 4 motors for movement in order to have better control of the robot's movement on the court and also to have the possibility of making a robot symmetrical both from the back and the front.
column35: We used a 6v and 10n solenoid as a base, modifying the central stem by cutting off both the back part and replacing the front part with a flat head screw to reduce its size so that it could fit in the space the robot has.
column36: We use two N20 motors connected by a gear train to a 15mm diameter rubber roller, mounted on two side bases with a bearing in the center so that the system can rotate on this axis, and we use elastic bands to make the dribbler exert pressure on the ball when making it spin.
column38: The dual kicker and dribbler system allows us to find and control the ball without needing to orbit it. This way, we can have better control of the ball on the field and thus direct it more easily towards the goal and score goals.
column41: Our robot one has a 12.6-volt battery, and our robot two has an 8.4-volt battery. Both robots use mini 560 regulators: one 5-volt regulator that powers all the logic, Arduino, sensors, cameras, and motor controllers, and one 3.3-volt regulator that powers only the LEDs of the line sensors.
column42: We use Monster Moto Shield motor controllers. These operate on the same 5-volt logic as the Arduino, so the connection is direct and allows us to vary the speed of each motor using PWM control, as well as the direction. These controllers support up to 30 amps peak, enough to run our motors.
column43: We use an Arduino Mega 2560 because it has a large number of digital pins and PWM with which we can control the motors, the dribbler system, the kicker system, sensors, it has both serial and I2C communication ports.
column44: We use mathematical functions to convert the coordinates of the ball transmitted by the camera to the controller and thus send data to each independent motor to generate movement towards the ball
column45: Our cameras work by detecting thresholds to identify the color of the ball and the goals, and thus return an x and y coordinate value to the controller.
column46: Our line circuits are photoreflection circuits in which, due to the reflected light emitted by white LEDs around them, an analog value is returned to each of the analog pins of the Arduino.
column47:
We use an mpu6050 sensor with a filtering chip to obtain real values without so much drift when the robot is in motion
We also use ultrasonic sensors to locate ourselves on the court or by using the walls as a reference.
column48: The system operates using a capacitor bank charged to 38 volts via a step-up converter and uses a logic reference MOSFET to actuate the solenoid.
column49: We control the motor in one direction using a DRV8833 motor driver
column52: Our electronics are compatible with most chips on the market that operate at 5V logic, but they can also be connected to 3.3V logic using other external boards that we made.
column54: We use color threshold detection from the vi cameras to send the x and y coordinates that the camera is detecting on the ball via uart to the Arduino
column55: We use two game logics: one for a player who follows the ball regardless of where he is positioned on the field, and one for a goalkeeper who follows the ball only while protecting his area and his goal.
column56: We use ultrasonic sensors to calculate the difference in distances between them in order to average a central distance of the robot and make it always return to the same specific area.
column57: We use a line detection algorithm by degrees to know where the line is being read in relation to the ball's tracking movement and thus identify whether the movement might cause the robot to go out of bounds or if it can continue performing it
column58: Our robot looks for the ball and when it can move it with the dribbler, it looks for the center of the goal and shoots to score the goal.
column59: Our goalkeeper prioritizes staying in his defensive area close to his goal without touching the golf area so that he can react quickly to an attack from the opposing team.
column60: N/A
column61: We have an algorithm which, upon detecting the ball and that it is in the drift area, is able to manipulate it and direct it towards the goal and kick it, thus making it more likely to score a goal.

column32: We used Onshape to design the robot, benchmarking top teams and our past data. To ensure match reliability, we focused on size rules (within 22x22 cm), stability (lowering center of mass), and modular layout for quick repairs. For improvements, we updated the fixed intake to an active, movable mechanism with dynamic joints to absorb impact, and added infrared sensors inside the mechanism to achieve precise, real-time closed-loop ball detection.
column33: We used 3D printed parts and outsourced plate cutting to www.fhrobotronics.com. We used JLCPCB for PCB manufacturing. During assembly, we adjusted CAD tolerances for smooth bearing fit and rerouted wires to avoid mechanical interference.
column34: We used 4 motors with 4 custom omni wheels for 360-degree holonomic movement. We designed our own wheels to optimize roller friction and maximize grip. Motor parts used: Maxon DC motors (Part No. 343194), details match our BOM.
column35: We used an off-the-shelf solenoid kicker mounted in a custom 3D printed frame. The plunger is triggered via an external high-voltage capacitor circuit for maximum impact force, and a mechanical spring ensures instant automatic reset after firing.
column36: We designed an active dribbler using a motor-driven roller wrapped in a high-friction silicone sleeve to spin and stabilize the ball. It features a movable suspension mechanism to absorb impacts, and built-in infrared sensors for real-time ball detection.
column38: We are most proud of our active, movable ball-capturing mechanism. Instead of using a fixed frame, we engineered dynamic joints with a flexible suspension system that adjusts its angle on the fly to absorb impacts and prevent the ball from bouncing away. Integrated with internal infrared sensors, this setup enables instant closed-loop feedback for ultra-precise, real-time ball control during high-speed maneuvers.
column41: Our robot uses two 12.6V batteries to isolate power. Battery 1 powers the main controller via a 5V buck converter. Battery 2 directly powers high-load components, including motors, the kicker module, and the ball-dribbler mechanism.
column42: Our robot uses an MD02 dual-channel motor driver with an H-bridge MOSFET architecture. Controlled via PWM/DIR/SLP pins from the microcontroller, it regulates speed and direction. The SLP (Sleep) pin enables control, allowing low-power standby or instant disabling for safety.
column43: Our robot utilizes two Teensy 4.1 boards in a Master-Slave configuration, chosen for their 600 MHz processing speed and abundant I/O pins. The Master Teensy acts as the central hub, processing inputs from infrared sensors, dual cameras, and ultrasonic sensors. The Slave Teensy focuses entirely on detecting white lines and executing motor control based on navigation data received from the Master board.
column44: To move our motors, the Master Teensy first calculates the target movement vector based on all sensor data. It then transmits this navigation data to the Slave Teensy via serial communication.
column45: For ball detection, our robot combines infrared sensors and a vision camera. For long-range tracking, TSSP4038 IR receivers detect the ball's pulsed IR signal, allowing the robot to locate and approach it quickly. Once close, the robot switches to a vision camera that identifies the ball's orange color and shape. This camera data is used to execute precise orbit and dribbling maneuvers.
column46: Our line detection system uses 32 pairs of photoresistors (3.3V) and LEDs (5V). Analog data is routed through CD74HC4067 multiplexers to save pins, serializing the readings for the Slave Teensy to perform real-time white line detection.
column47: For positioning, our robot uses an omni mirror camera system to detect angles and distances to goals for precise field coordinates. This is complemented by HC-SR04 ultrasonic sensors for wall distances and a BNO085 IMU to track heading. All connect to the Master Teensy: the BNO085 via I2C/SPI, ultrasonic sensors via digital GPIO, and the omni mirror via a serial (UART) interface.
column48: Our kicker system runs on an independent circuit. It steps up 12V power to 48V using a boost converter to charge the capacitors. To kick, the Master Teensy sends a digital signal that triggers a fast-switching circuit, instantly discharging the 48V energy into the solenoid.
column49: Our dribbler system uses an 1806 BLDC motor to control the ball, powered directly from the power board. Its electronic speed controller (ESC) receives a PWM control signal from the Master Teensy to activate, spin, and regulate the motor's speed for optimal ball handling.
column52:
A major technical innovation of our robot is the implementation of a dual-circuit power isolation architecture that completely separates the sensitive logic control system from the high-current actuator electronics.

In traditional single-battery setups, heavy motor loads, kicker discharges, and dribbler accelerations frequently cause massive voltage drops (brownouts) and electromagnetic interference (EMI), leading to microcontroller instability or sudden system resets.

To solve this, our design utilizes two independent 12.6V battery packs to achieve absolute electrical isolation. The first battery serves as the logic power supply, dropping safely to 5V via a high-efficiency buck converter to provide clean, stable power exclusively for the Master and Slave Teensy boards and their sensor networks. The second battery operates on a dedicated high-load circuit, delivering raw, un-regulated power directly to the MD02 motor drivers, the 48V kicker boost converter, and the 1806 BLDC dribbler ESC.

This hardware-level separation ensures that high-frequency switching noise and sudden current spikes from the actuators never propagate back into the processing logic. The result is a highly resilient, noise-immune system capable of executing high-speed maneuvers and rapid kicking sequences with zero risk of processor brownouts or control loop latency.
column54: To locate the ball, the TSSP4038 infrared sensors capture pulsed IR signals, which are pre-processed by an ESP32 microcontroller and transmitted to the Master Teensy via UART serial communication. Simultaneously, for close-range tracking, a MaixCam vision camera detects the orange ball and transmits the processed coordinate data directly to the Master Teensy via a separate UART interface.
column55: Our algorithm calculates the optimal intercept path using the ball's distance and angle. The Striker uses an orbiting algorithm to loop behind the ball and push it toward the opponent's goal, while the Goalie uses linear tracking to move sideways, staying between the ball and our goal to block shots.
column56: Our localization algorithm utilizes the omni mirror system to detect the angles and distances to both goals, converting these visual landmarks into a precise X and Y coordinate system of the field. We use these coordinates to track our exact position in real-time, primarily utilizing this data to trigger out-of-bounds containment and ensure the robot stays within safe playing boundaries.
column57: Our robot tracks white boundary lines using a photoresistor array. Our out-of-bounds avoidance algorithm is based on vector addition: when multiple sensors detect a line, the system aggregates their positions as vectors, calculates the net resultant angle, and commands the robot to move in the exact opposite vector direction to safely back away.
column58: Our goal-scoring algorithm relies on a dedicated front-facing camera to locate the opponent's goal. Once the robot successfully secures possession of the ball, the algorithm immediately calculates the angle to the target goal, commands the robot to rotate and face the goal, and initiates a high-speed sprint straight toward it to execute a powerful shot.
column59: Our Goalie uses a linear tracking algorithm, shifting laterally to stay between the ball and our net to block shots. Meanwhile, the Striker acts as the first line of defense, orbiting to cut off opponent angles and strip possession. To ensure stability, the system fuses omni mirror and ultrasonic data to keep the Goalie perfectly positioned without colliding with the posts.
column60: We don't have communication.
column61: I am most proud of our Vector-Based Out-of-Bounds Avoidance Algorithm. When the photoresistor array detects the boundary line, it maps the triggered sensors as spatial vectors to calculate a net resultant vector, commanding the robot to back away in the exact opposite direction. This eliminates boundary stalling and allows the robot to retreat smoothly while keeping its front face locked onto the ball for instant counter-attacks.

column32: We used Fusion 360 to design our mechanical parts and modify specific components, such as the omniwheel mounts and TSSP sensor covers. Since many parts connect together and face strong contact during matches, they had to be both strong and lightweight to stay under the weight limit. We also designed parts with multiple purposes; for example, the front guard protects the robot from collisions while also connecting the top and bottom layers.
column33: We used a A1 3d printer to print the parts we had previously designed. Our PCBs where made externally, the company that made the PCB is JLCPCB.
column34: Omnidirectional movement. With 4 omni-wheels arranged at 45° angles, the robot can move in any direction instantly without rotating first. 3 or other amounts of wheels can also achieve this, but 4 gives better force distribution, each wheel contributes more evenly when strafing.
column35: Our robot uses a premade solenoid. The specific solenoid is made by Takaha and the model is 10Ω(CB10370100)
column36: N/A
column38: Im most proud of the design for connecting our solenoid to the bracket that holds it. This design took along time and many iterations to get right.
column41: Our robot uses a 3-cell lithium-ion battery pack, which provides up to 12.6 V when fully charged. A regulator steps this down to 5 V for the motor drivers, IMU/gyroscope, ultrasonic sensors, and other electronics, while a 3.3 V LDO powers the STM32 microcontrollers.
column42: All the motor drivers have a clock line and a data line, this allows the proccesor to send signals to all the drivers.
column43: The STM32G030C8T6 is used on the sensor boards because it is low-cost and has enough GPIO and ADC resources for reading sensors. The STM32F407VET6 is used as the main controller because it has higher speed, more memory, and better communication features for movement and decision-making.
column44: The proccessor communictates with the motor driver which then makes the motor spin. All the motors have a clock line and a data line, these are all connected together. When the proccessor wants to talk to a specific motor it will use it's id(0,1,2,3).
column45: The ball emits a 40Hz square wave, so each sensor only reads 0 if it detects the ball and 1 if not. Since the signal does not show distance, our robot uses 36 sensors spaced 10° apart. The algorithm finds the largest group of sensors reading 1, including wrap-around groups, then calculates the group’s center to find the ball’s angle. The size of the group estimates distance, allowing the robot to locate the ball.
column46: Our robot uses 55 LED-phototransistor boundary sensors. Each LED shines light at the floor, and the phototransistor reads the reflection. A brighter reflection means the sensor sees the white boundary line, so the main board detects which side crossed and moves the robot away.
column47: Our robot uses TSSP sensors, ultrasonic sensors, a gyroscope, and phototransistors. TSSPs detect the ball and guide movement. Four ultrasonic sensors measure distance to the walls for LocationX and LocationY, helping the goalie stay near the goal. The gyroscope tracks angle changes, while LED and phototransistor pairs detect white boundary lines.
column48: We have a booster that changes our power output from 12.6V to 47V. It sends the 47V to the kicker driver which then uses the high voltage to power the kicker. The kicker driver connects to the output of the driver to the transistor which allows us to send commands to activate the kicker.
column49: N/A
column52: We are most proud of the ball sensor board/main proccessor board because, previously we had two separate boards, one for proccessing and one for sensing the ball. We tried really hard to intergrate these two because combinning these two reduces our weight by quite a bit.
column54: We find the ball with 36 TSSP sensors around the robot. Each sensor reads 1 when it detects the ball’s 40Hz signal and 0 when it does not. Since the signal is only on/off, it cannot directly show distance. Instead, we estimate distance from the size of the largest group of sensors reading 1: a wider group usually means the ball is closer.
column55: We make a projection point behind the ball since we always want to catch the ball facing the opposing team's net. We use the angle of the ball and the robot's coordinates in the field to determine where the projection point is. The robot will then try to move to that projection without hitting the ball
column56: We use four ultrasonic sensors to estimate the robot’s position on the field. The left and right sensors calculate LocationX, while the front and back sensors calculate LocationY using distance from the walls. The gyroscope tracks the robot’s angle, keeping movement consistent. Phototransistors detect white boundary lines so the robot can move away and stay inside them.
column57: Our robot uses 55 boundary sensors around the base and center. Each LED shines light at the floor, and the phototransistor picks up the reflected light. A brighter reflection means a white line is detected. The main board finds which where its triggered, then counts the consecutive sensors as the Push value, more sensors means a stronger move away.
column58: We use ball sensors to detect the ball and move toward it. When the ball is at the front of the robot near 0°, we check if it is inside the front cutout using an LDR sensor. Once the LDR confirms we have the ball, the robot uses ultrasonic sensor data to aim, then activates the kicker to shoot.
column59: At the start of the game we assign one robot to be the defender, the deffender uses the ball sensors(TSSPs) to detect the ball. After it comes in to a certain distance the deffender will move towards the ball and kick it away. This motion is also to block the opposing team's robot from scoring. After kicking the ball away it uses the data from the ultrasonic sensors to move back infront of the net
column60: To connect with each other we use bluetooth V2, we have a HC05 module on each robot which allows the robots to communicate with each other. The most important part of this connection is so that, when one robot is out side of the boundary line the other will take on the position of that robot e.g. attacker becomes defender and vice versa.
column61: We are very proud of our ball detection algorithm(see above for more), after the change we initially were going to cameras to detect the ball. But then we discovered that we could use the cluster method! We are so proud to discover this method since its so much faster and more accurate then using a camera.
