column32: We used Fusion360 to design the 3D CAD for the shields, dribbler, and light gate for our robot, then printed them using a BambuLab printer. We implemented a dribbler this year. We also improved our design from last year by adding flexible TPU shields, which absorbs collision shocks better than our previous shields. Additionally, we experimented with the design of our ball capture zone to have a rounded yet wide enough area for the robots to control the ball effectively.
column33: We used KiCad to design our PCBs. We then sent these new designs to JLC PCB to manufacture them. Much of our robot's body is built of 3D printed parts. We designed these parts on Fusion 360, and then used a Bambu A1 to print them with PLA and TPU.
column34: We are using four 9.7:1 Pololu DC motors (Pololu item #4842) on each of our robots. We decided to stick with a four-wheel drivetrain over a three-wheel one because it provided us with good stability, weight distribution, and traction. We use 50mm omniwheels from GTFRobots. The links to these parts are in our BOM. We bought the motors with encoders attached for localization purposes.
column35: This year, we opted to not use a kicker due to weight constraints. Instead, we innovated a bidirectional dribbler that will launch the ball by reversing the dribbler direction, acting as a kicker. We experimented with different torques and RPM to balance grip and speed.
column36: Our back dribbler uses a 1000kV drone motor and a pulley system in order to capture the new smaller orange ball. We also implemented a new innovative bidirectional ESC, allowing us to use our dribbler as a kicker. We use a backspin strategy to capture the ball and flick it across the field.
column38: After experimenting with different materials for our shields, we decided to use TPU because it provides better shock absorption. This minimizes any damage to the PCB caused by collisions. This was also the first year we implemented a dribbler, which went through more than ten iterations to be as light-weight and space-efficient as possible. We tested different drone motors and combinations of torque and RPM so that the robot could hold and launch the ball effectively.
column41: Our robot has a 12V battery that powers the 4 DC motors, dribbler drone motor, motor drivers, and 5V and 3.3V buck converters. The 5V then goes to the Teensy 4.1, IR sensors, and camera, while the 3.3V powers the light, orientation, and ultrasonic sensors. We use a MOSFET to regulate the voltage.
column42: We drive four motors, each through its own Pololu High-Power Simple Motor Controller G2 18v15 (#1363), commanded by the Teensy over serial. The controllers run on 12V from the battery and drive four 9.7:1 Metal Gearmotor 25Dx63L mm HP 12V motors with 48 CPR encoders (#4842).
column43: We chose to use a Teensy 4.1 microcontroller because it has a large number of pins that support all of our sensors. It also supports many protocols, including I2C, which allows us to connect multiple sensors to a single port. Although it is somewhat fragile and damages more easily than other boards, we decided that the Teensy is convenient and lightweight enough to outweigh these drawbacks. Our robot only uses one processor, but we may consider more in the future for more analog ports.
column44: The Teensy 4.1 drives our four motors over serial ports. We compute wheel speeds with sin/cos, then send speed/direction(0-3200) to four motor drivers. Those drivers generate the PWM, rapidly switching each motor's H-bridge on/off–duty cycle sets speed, a forward/reverse byte sets direction.
column45:
Our robots use two IR compound eyes, each with seven ports, mounted on the left and right sides for full 360° vision. Each sensor returns an IR strength reading: where we take the vector sum of the top 5 ports to triangulate the ball’s position.

Our robots also carry a M5 Stack camera that differentiates objects by color including the orange ball. This tracks both the ball and our position on the field, letting us judge if the ball is in our capture zone and how our current position.
column46: The baseplate PCB ring connects 48 light sensors that detect white lines. Three 74HC4067 16-channel analog multiplexers combine their outputs into far fewer Teensy inputs, cutting pin count and wiring complexity. A 10-pin JST routes power and signal to the second plate, which holds the Teensy.
column47: Many sensors work to locate the robot. Four URM13 sensors measure the distance from the walls to find our position on the field. A BNO055 compass tracks rotation to prevent us from overturning. Light sensors keep us from crossing the bounds, and let the robot follow the line on defense. Cameras locate us relative to the goal so we can drive toward its center to score.
column48: The Teensy sends a PWM pulse to a bidirectional ESC powering the dribbler motor, setting direction and speed: above neutral the wheel spins one way to grip the ball and hold it. When ready, the ESC reverses below neutral, spinning the wheel the opposite way to fling the ball forward and launch it.
column49: Our dribbler is a drone motor driven by a bidirectional ESC powered by the 12V battery. The Teensy controls it with a PWM signal on pin 12, whose pulse width sets both speed and direction: one way grips the ball with backspin to hold it, the reverse flings it forward to launch.
column52:
The electronics we're proudest of is our baseplate light-sensor board. Beyond the 48-sensor line-detection ring, two choices make it reliable in any venue and at any battery level: a potentiometer that tunes the array to the room's lighting, and a MOSFET circuit that regulates the voltage feeding it.

1. The potentiometer: adapting to lighting in seconds
Our 48 downward-facing sensors find the white border by reflected light: white reflects a lot, green carpet little. But ambient light varies by venue, pushing every reading too high (all white) or too low (no line). So we put a potentiometer on the PCB that sets the array's operating point. One knob shifts all 48 sensors into the usable middle of our analog-to-digital range, where green and white separate cleanly. It's an instant field adjustment, no laptop or re-flashing.

The innovation is pairing this analog trim with software calibration. The pot gets the array into its sweet spot; then we press calibrate and sweep every sensor over the green carpet. The robot records each sensor's strongest green reading, sets an individual threshold, and saves all 48 to non-volatile memory. Dead sensors are detected and locked out so they can't false-trigger. The pot handles the global offset and the firmware handles each sensor's sensitivity, staying robust without re-tuning code at every competition. (Three 16-channel chips multiplex all 48 sensors down to a few pins.)

2. The MOSFET voltage regulation: a stable rail under load
The pot setting only stays meaningful because a MOSFET circuit regulates the voltage to the sensors. Our battery isn't a stiff 12V: a LiPo sags over a match and droops hard when all four motors pull current at once. If the sensors rode that noisy motor rail, readings would drift and our start-of-match thresholds would go wrong. So we use MOSFETs to hold a clean, regulated rail, decoupled from motor power. Their very low on-resistance switches the load efficiently with little wasted heat, unlike a linear regulator. That keeps the rail constant regardless of battery state or load, so the saved thresholds stay valid the whole match. Those three layers (regulated voltage, pot trim, and per-sensor calibration) make line detection that doesn't drift as the battery drains, which is what we're most proud of.
column54: Two IR sensors, one on each side of the robot, give a full 360° view to spot the ball. Each port has seven IR sensors that return a strength reading. We compute the direction vector using the top x readings from the ports, where we can determine with higher accuracy compared to just taking the port with higher strength.
column55: Our striker chases the ball but doesn't drive straight at it: it curls along an arc, the steering offset growing as the ball sits more to its side, so it sweeps around and traps the ball facing the goal. Our goalie does the opposite. It hugs the white goal line and only slides left/right to stay between the ball and the net, never chasing into the field. The ball just picks which way it slides.
column56:
Four ultrasonic sensors measure our distance to each wall, telling us where we sit on the field. Our airwall algorithm feeds those distances into a virtual force field: it splits our drive into x/y parts and, as a wall nears, scales that part down then reverses it. This curves the robot away from walls and the out-of-bounds line while it keeps chasing the ball.
https://youtu.be/iRhOrAF4o7Y
column57:
A ring of 48 light sensors spots the white line. Each sensor that sees white contributes a unit vector in its direction; we sum these into one vector pointing toward the line, clustering segments to handle corners. The robot then drives the opposite way to escape back inbounds. Our ultrasonic air wall backs this up, curving us off the walls before we reach the line.
https://youtu.be/bMGGJdDkwOE
column58:
Once we have the ball and we’re close to the edge of the field, we do backspin strategy. The robot backs up and spins in place to fling it toward the goal. If we have the ball and we’re in the middle of the field, our camera finds the goal by its color, locks onto its center, and we drive straight at that angle to carry the ball in and score. 
https://youtu.be/qSJhCxreypY
column59: Our goalie hugs the white goal line, sliding left/right to stay between the ball and the net instead of chasing into the field. We designed and tuned this line-following algorithm with our own Python physics simulator: you drive the robot with arrow keys and it returns a target direction and speed. Anyone can run it: https://github.com/jscwang/2026-RoboNYC/tree/master/Python-Sim
column60: We used an HC05 Bluetooth system to switch defense to offense when the offense robot gets turned off; We also use it to localize the position of the ball, if the defense robot can’t see the orange ball with its camera.

column32: We designed our robot using Fusion 360 for mechanical parts and EasyEDA for PCBs. Sensor placement was based on research and testing: ultrasonic sensors were mounted on a lowered top plate for accurate wall detection, IR sensors were positioned at ball height, and the camera and mirror were placed using calculated dimensions. Future improvements include a light-gate ball detector and a curved mirror for wider vision, improving ball detection and aiming accuracy.
column33: PCBs were designed by us and manufactured by JLCPCB. Mirror and ultrasonic sensor mounts were designed in Fusion 360 and 3D-printed. Mounting hole and motor positioning issues were resolved by redesigning mounts and adjusting component placement.
column34: We used 4 Joinmax 3561 motors for each robot, controlled by 2 Cytron MDD10A motor drivers. This achieved a 4-wheel drive base, enabling rapid changes in direction & independent control of rotational motion. We used omnidirectional wheels on each motor. This allows for manoeuvrable & responsive movement, faster response in gaining possession of the ball, & preventing the opposing team from scoring.
column35: We designed the kicker circuit in EasyEDA. A TC4420 gate driver drives an IRF3205 MOSFET, enabling our microcontroller to switch the 11.1V, high-current solenoid. An SR360 flyback diode and parallel capacitor suppress back-EMF and electrical noise, protecting the circuit.
column38: We are most proud of our custom PCBs and design process. Instead of using loose wiring, we designed PCBs in EasyEDA to connect the controllers, sensors, and power distribution in one system. We improved the layout through several iterations, focusing on component placement, routing, and access. This made the robot easier to assemble and test, as well as reduced wiring errors during operation.
column41: Our robots use an 11.1V battery, regulated to 5V to power our primary and secondary controllers, the Raspberry Pi Pico and ESP32. The motor drivers and solenoid are powered directly by the 11.1V battery to provide sufficient current and voltage for reliable motor performance and kicking strength.
column42: Our robot uses dual H-bridge motor drivers to control the DC motors. The Raspberry Pi Pico sends PWM and direction signals to the drivers, allowing precise speed and direction control. The motor drivers are powered directly by the 11.1V battery for sufficient power output.
column43: We used a Raspberry Pi Pico as the primary controller due to its dual-core processor and PIO blocks, enabling fast real-time processing of data from the compass, camera, ultrasonic sensors and IR compound eyes for responsive navigation. An ESP32 acts as a secondary controller, continuously sampling TEMT6000 phototransistors using its ADCs for rapid white-line detection. Separating these tasks prevents processing bottlenecks and improves overall robot performance throughout matches.
column44: The Pico is connected to the motor drivers via gpio analog. Each motor is controlled with a speed and a direction pin. For the speed pin, To drive at a certain angle, the speed of each of the motors required is calculated using trigonometry, as they are mounted at 45 degree angles around the robot.
column45: We used two compound eyes mounted at the front and rear of the robot to achieve 360° ball detection. They communicate via I2C using the TwoWire library. As both sensors share the same address, they are connected to separate I2C buses. Each compound eye has seven channels that report IR intensity. The strongest signal and its neighbouring channels are interpolated to calculate the ball’s angle precisely. This serves as our primary ball detection system, without relying on the camera.
column46: Our line detection circuit uses a TEMT6000 sensor and LED. The LED illuminates the field and the sensor measures reflected light. White lines reflect more light than the green field, producing a higher voltage. The Pico reads this analogue signal and detects the boundary with a calibrated threshold.
column47: We use ultrasonic sensors to determine the position of the robot on the field. To ensure that the robot is always facing the front so as to capture the ball with the ballcap, a compass is utilised. The target angle is always set to 0 degrees, and the error is calculated. This is used to rotate the robot to the front.
column48: A boost converter charges a capacitor from the 12 V battery. When commanded, the Pico triggers a TC4420 gate driver, switching an N-channel MOSFET to discharge the capacitor through the solenoid. This creates a magnetic field that launches the plunger to kick the ball.
column52:
Although we used a commercially available solenoid, we designed the electronics required to integrate it into our robot reliably and efficiently. A custom driver circuit allows the microcontroller to safely control the high current required by the solenoid, while a capacitor bank stores energy and rapidly discharges it to deliver a powerful, consistent kick. The circuit was optimised to recharge quickly after each shot, enabling repeated kicks during matches. Protection components were also included to prevent voltage spikes from damaging the electronics, ensuring reliable operation throughout competitions.

We designed custom PCBs to integrate all of our robot’s electronics into a compact and reliable system. Rather than relying on jumper wires and prototype boards, each PCB was tailored to our robot’s layout and electrical requirements. Dedicated connectors for sensors, motors and communication buses simplify assembly and maintenance, while separating power and signal traces reduces electrical interference. Wider high-current traces safely handle the motor and kicker circuits, resulting in a cleaner, more robust and competition-ready electronics system.
column54: We used 2 compound eyes at the front & rear for 360° ball detection. They communicate via I2C with the TwoWire library. As both share the same address, they are connected to separate I2C buses. Each sensor has 7 IR channels. By interpolating the strongest signal with its adjacent channels, the robot calculates the ball angle more precisely, providing reliable tracking without camera reliance.
column55: Instead of driving directly at the ball, which could knock it away, our robot orbits the ball to approach from behind. Four movement cases are defined based on the ball’s position. The drive angle is calculated from the ball angle plus a scaled offset based on the maximum IR intensity, while orbit speed is also scaled using IR intensity for smoother, more reliable ball capture.
column56: We use 4 ultrasonic sensors to measure the robot’s distance from each wall, allowing its coordinates on the field to be calculated. These coordinates enable movement to target positions, such as the field centre or along the goal line. Using basic trigonometry, the robot calculates the required heading, while its speed is scaled according to the remaining distance for smooth, accurate navigation.
column57: To detect field boundaries, reflected light sensors identify the contrast between green and white surfaces. Arranged in a ring, they let the robot move away when a line is detected. Four ultrasonic sensors estimate field position by measuring wall distances. If near a wall or localisation confidence is low due to blocked sensors, the robot slows to improve navigation and accuracy.
column58: If the IR intensity is greater than a certain value and the ball angle is within a certain range, (e.g. 345-15 degrees), the ball is considered within the ball capture zone. The robot accelerates and faces the goal, delivering a kick to score.
column59: The goalie remains at the goal line (along a certain y coordinate), and follows the ball such that it is always directly in front of it. If the ball is within the ball capture zone after stopping a goal, the goalie switches to a striker and brings the ball towards the centre.
column61: One software innovation we are most proud of is developing the robot’s autonomous navigation and ball-tracking system. We fused data from infrared ball sensors, ultrasonic localisation, the compass, goal camera and boundary sensors. We implemented interpolation to estimate ball angles more accurately, confidence-based localisation, predictive movement calculations, dynamic ball orbiting for improved shooting angles, and adaptive speed control, boosting match performance.

column32: Most mechanical parts were designed in Autodesk Fusion 360. PCBs were designed in Fusion and finalized in KiCad. Our iterative design focused on precision, robustness, speed, maneuverability, and intelligence, using brushless motors, an effective dribbler, a powerful kicker, and a 360° vision system with two 220° lenses and AI. The dribbler underwent the most iterations, testing different rollers, damping, rotation axes, capture zones, materials, and 3D-printed parts.
column33: Built mainly by 3D printing with Bambu Lab A1/A1 mini. PCBs by JLCPCB. Omniwheels developed with EK Haan and made by ACROSS Tooling. This season: 2 cameras replaced the mirror, new motor layout, wider dribbler, new line sensor PCB.
column34: Our drive system uses four axially symmetric motors, with front motors at 60° and rear motors at 135°, inspired by SSL robots to improve forward power and create more space for a wider dribbler. The omnidirectional wheels feature four aluminum plates, one spring steel plate, and 24 offset rubber rollers with notches for better traction. A fifth motor drives the dribbler.
column35: A custom-wound 24 V solenoid with a 10 mm nominal stroke serves as the kicker. It uses 6 layers of 0.5 mm copper wire. A 24 V capacitor stabilizes the power supply during kicks. The enlarged, curved contact surface centers the ball and improves accuracy.
column36: Our dribbler uses a 16:1 geared motor and a brass to steel 1.6:1 gear stage. Weight distribution and a rear-bottom bearing create cushioned natural suspension. A 12.8 mm OD silicone tube, bottom slope, and floor contact guide the ball via three contact points, minimizing bounce and ball loss.
column38: Our standout feature is our dribbler. It allows us to control the ball much better and keep hold of it more effectively. As a result, we can score significantly more goals and lose possession less often.
column41: Three 8.2V battery packs provide 24.6V, stabilized by a capacitor during kicks. Motors and drivers use 24V, while converters supply 3.3V and 5V. The 3.3V rail powers ESPs, light barrier, buttons, floor sensors, and communication; 5V powers the Raspberry Pi, fans, and cameras.
column42: We use the Maxon ECX Flat 22S motor with the planetary gear of the GMP22-180SH motor. Those are controlled by ESCON2 Nano 24/2 motor drivers which are mounted on our main PCB and powered with 24V.
column43:
The robot is controlled by two microprocessors and a Raspberry Pi.
The Pi handles the vision system, specifically reading and processing data from the cameras.
The first microprocessor (ESP32-WROOM-32) is responsible for processing input from the ground sensor, while the second, acting as the main processor (ESP32 Dev Module), coordinates all other components and sensors and determines the direction of travel based on the available information.
column44: We move our motors using the ESP32 dev Module on our Main PCB. Each ESCON2 Nano 24/2 motor driver receives a PWM, direction and enable signal from our ESP32 which allows us to control the speed and direction of the motors.
column45:
We are using two ArduCams B0428 with a wide-angle lens (220°) in combination with a Raspberry Pi 5 and a Hailo-8L accelerator.
To detect the ball, we trained our own YOLOv8n-seg model, which predicts which pixels in the image represent the ball.
After that, we calculate the center of the blob and undistort these coordinates. From this, we determine the angle (using arctan2) and the distance to the ball.
column46: Our floor sensor board uses 48 sensors and 48 white LEDs, connected via six multiplexers to a 3.3V ESP32-WROOM. The ESP32 processes the data to calculate the exact line direction as a vector and transmits it to the main controller via I2C.
column47: Navigation relies solely on our camera system running on a Raspberry Pi 5, connected to the ESP32 via UART. The ESP32 calculates vectors to both goals and derives the robot’s heading from them. As all objects are detected relative to the robot, it has no absolute position on the field.
column48: Our custom-wound kicker uses thick wire to handle currents above 50 A. A high-current FET and an Infineon BTN8982 half-bridge with integrated driver enable direct ESP32 control. A 22,000 µF capacitor supplies the required current for the 12 ms kick.
column49: We use a motor with a 16:1 gearbox. The 3D-printed dribbler has two gears (1.6:1). Weight distribution and a rear-bottom bearing create cushioned suspension stopped by the upper wall. A 12.8 mm silicone tube and slope ensure 3-point guidance. Driven by an ESCON2 Nano 24/2 via custom PCB.
column52:
We are most proud of designing a new line detection sensor. This board is equipped with 48 sensors and 48 white LEDs, instead of 32 like last year, connected to an ESP32-WROOM via six multiplexers and powered by 3.3V.
All data is processed on the ESP32 to calculate a vector indicating the exact direction of the line. This information is then transmitted to our main controller via I2C.
column54: We trained our own YOLOv8n-seg model, which predicts which pixels in the image are presenting the ball. After we calculate the center of the blob and undistort those coordinates, we can determine an angle and a distance to the ball which is then send via UART to our main ESP32.
column55: We use two ball-catching algorithms. The striker approaches the ball along an arc using an angle- and distance-dependent offset vector that shrinks as alignment improves. The keeper drives between ball and goal on an elliptical path, preventing it from getting stuck on the goal line while maintaining fast reactions to block shots.
column56: We only use the cameras for navigation and positioning. For each goal, we created a vector which allows us to calculate a midpoint of the field and an orientation of the robot. All those vectors are relative to the robot, so we do not have an absolute position on the field. But through angles and distances, we can assume where the robot should be and act accordingly.
column57: Our custom PCB uses 48 line sensors. Sensor values are grouped into consecutive “clouds”, whose average angles are calculated before determining the final line angle. This approach is more robust against noise and detects line crossings reliably. To prevent overshooting, robot speed is reduced based on its distance from the field center, giving the motors enough time to react.
column58: We have different algorithms which are all triggered when the ball is detected by our light barrier and depending on the angle of the ball. We use the dribbler mostly to get the ball out of the corners and to prevent losing it during rotations towards the goal. If the angle to the midpoint of the goal is small enough, the robot kicks the ball, which is much quicker than driving.
column59: Our keeper moves on an elliptical path between the ball and our goal, maximizing goal coverage. Its rear camera always faces the goal center, ensuring the best attacking position when the ball approaches. Driving speed is adapted to the ball’s velocity and the distance to the next target point on the ellipse.
column60: We use a striker and a keeper communicating via ESP-NOW on the ESP32 (2.4 GHz, 100 mW EIRP). They switch roles dynamically: if one robot leaves the field, the other becomes the striker. The keeper also switches when it has a better chance to score. This communication ensures reliable coordination throughout the game.
column61: This year, we are most proud of our camera system. We changed from object detection to instance segmentation, which allows us to detect other robots on the field. Due to that, we can block our own goal much better and also kick more precisely into the biggest open gap in the goal.

column15: Our mentors supported us maintaining our working environment, as well as processing the required paperwork and accommodation for each competition.
column16: We have been a member of the same club activity for more than 3 years, and we recognized the technical strength of each member. We distributed tasks to maximize each member’s strength, and we catch up progress both online and offline.
column17: We utilized AI tools such as ChatGPT, Codex, and DeepL. We found that AI is effective for surveying supply chains.
column32: We designed the mechanical parts using Autodesk Fusion. We utilized the FEM of Autodesk Fusion to optimize the stiffness of each part. We performed thorough review in each component selection, including each screw to prevent the lack of fastener strength, the pressure area of the omniwheel’s roller, and the dribbler shaft, to achieve the well balanced rigidity while maximizing the maintainability.
column33:
We used 3D printing for large and complex 3D parts, and CNC milling for aluminum parts.
The hyperbolic mirror required a special process, which combines the smooth 3D mold made by JLCPCB, applied with the heated PVC mirror to shape the mirror.
column34: To achieve the best agility, we chose four omniwheels each driven by maxon RE16 with 19:1 gearhead as a drive system. Four omniwheels placed every 90 degrees have a stable moving force gradient than three wheels, yet better weight performance than five or more wheels. Since no commercial omniwheel satisfies our needs, we designed a high grip silicone rubber omniwheels and built them in-house.
column35: We chose the Takaha CB1037 solenoid for its stroke and driving force, and applied short-duration overdrive to obtain sufficient kicking force. The kicker plane was refined through iterative design to prevent the ball from entering the holding area and to avoid off-center impacts on the ball.
column36: Our robot equips a cylinder shaped dribble covered with silicone rubber to grip the ball. The roller is driven by a DC motor through the gear. It has a spring loaded pivot mechanism that provides robust ball capturing, and the hard stop prevents the excessive motion that loses the stability.
column38: A standout feature of our mechanical design is the dribbler, optimized for pivot range and spring load to maximize stability. During testing, we faced issues with the robot losing control of the ball. Hypothesizing that high-frequency vibrations were causing this, we revised the pivot joint tolerances and adjusted the upper limit. This fixed the issue, resulting in a dribbling mechanism that offers exceptional stability alongside remarkable agility.
column41: The robot has a battery voltage of 11.1-12.6 V as a primary, 45 V as a kicker power, and 3.3 and 5 V as a logic voltage. The battery voltage is directly supplied to the motor and subsystems through the fuse. We use XL6019 for 45 V, OKL-T/6-W12N-C DC-DC for 5V, and LCD’s built-in regulator for 3.3V.
column42: We use two DRV8412 motor drivers which enables the speed and direction of each of the four drive motors. Motor current can be monitored by an MCU through the built-in shunt resistor. This current monitoring allows detecting the robot being stuck or pushing against another robot.
column43: The robot has five MCUs or modules. Our multi MCU design allocates each microcontroller to each timing critical processing such as pulse width monitoring of ultrasonic or pulse ball sensors. The system centers STM32F446RE which has 6 UARTs, providing the communication to all other MCUs. We chose PIC32MK1024MCM100 as a line sensor MCU for its larger number of inputs, OpenMV H7 as a camera MCU, and the XIAO ESP32S3 as an inter-robot communication MCU.
column44: The main MCU STM32F446RE generates a couple of PWM signals for each motor, and is directly supplied to the DRV8412 motor driver. The motor is controlled by LAP (Locked Anti-Phase) PWM, which prevents the overshoot of the robot by inertia by braking the motor while downsloping the PWM.
column45:
For ball detection, we developed circularly placed 24x TSSP58038 IR sensors enclosed by a 3D printed screen. The pulse width obtained from each sensor is processed by SMA over a fixed time, then 2D projected based on each sensor's mounting angle, which determines the relative direction to the ball.
We smooth the number of responding sensors with a first-order filter and use that value as an indicator of relative distance.
column46: The circuit detects the line by the LEDs and phototransistor pair measures the light intensity. All the voltage levels are converted to digital signals,enabling easy and high speed processing. We designed both LED and phototransistor thresholds fully adjustable via LDO and DAC for game robustness.
column47: The robot has three ultrasonic sensors and an omnidirectional camera for navigation. The omnidirectional camera determines the relative angle, width, and distance of blue and yellow goals. To keep the robot heading to one direction, we use Euler angles obtained from a BNO055.
column48: The kicker circuit uses a boost converter XL6019 capable of generating up to 48V, and stores the energy in a 2200 uF capacitor. Stored energy will be bypassed through a FET and drives solenoid, generating the force to kick the ball.
column49: The dribbler circuit uses a boost converter XL6019 to generate up to 28 V, and drives the dribbler motor through a TB67H450 motor driver. We limit and monitor the current  from the MCU as we apply a higher than the rated voltage. This achieves a higher speed while preventing the heat damage.
column52: The main improvements in our electronics are white-line sensing with comparators and LED brightness control that can be adjusted from software. The line-sensor board has 60 sensors, so reading every sensor individually with an ADC would make each cycle longer. In an oscilloscope measurement comparing the delay to a binary sensor result, the comparator output in this circuit became available about 32 us earlier than ADC readout. Across all 60 sensors, this corresponds to about 2 ms per readout cycle. At a maximum speed of 1.6 m/s, the robot travels 3 mm in 2 ms. Since the white line is 20 mm wide, this difference may affect line-detection response, so fast detection with comparators is useful. We also generate the reference voltage from the MCU's DAC, which makes threshold configurable from software. The LED brightness is also configurable by  software, enabled by LDO feedback circuit combined with a digital potentiometer. This allows both the threshold and illumination level to be changed without hardware adjustment, and can support automatic setting of the sensor reference voltage.
column54: We calculate the ball direction by averaging the detection pulse width from each IR sensor over a fixed time, then synthesizing those values as vectors on a plane based on the mounting angle of each sensor. We do not directly measure the distance to the ball. Instead, we smooth the number of responding sensors with a first-order filter and use it as an indicator of ball closeness.
column55: Strategically, a forward robot approaches the ball to capture and dribble to the opponent’s goal. The target movement angles are stored as a look-up table, read and join them as a spline curve,  enabling a smoother trajectory. The algorithm prioritizes approaching to the ball when the ball is far, and gradually biased to track the goal as the ball gets closer.
column56: The robot does not use absolute position estimation, but switches behavior using an omnidirectional camera and ultrasonic sensors. The camera, combined with a hyperbolic mirror, provides goal-related information for various strategies. The ultrasonic sensors measure wall distance to adjust ball movement and serve as a fallback when the goals cannot be detected.
column57: The robot detects the field lines by a 32x light intensity sensor placed circularly around the center, and additional sensors on the left, right, and rear edge. It calculates the closest point on the line, reverses the resulting vector, and moves along it at a constant speed to stay inside the field.
column58: Our algorithm keeps dribbling and kicking the ball to the opponent’s goal, not only when the ball is in the playing field, but also when the ball is outside of the playing field on either side of the opponent's goal. The color signature of the goal is used to determine the target shooting angle, enabling the dodging of the opponent's robot and increasing the scoring accuracy.
column59: The robot assigned the goalkeeper role defends the goal by tracing the penalty area line while moving laterally to the ball. This blocks the incoming shots, including the friendly bounceback. The goalkeeper algorithm also utilizes the color signature from the omnidirectional camera, estimating the relative position from the own goal, preventing the off-trace.
column60: By using communication, the robots can switch roles more flexibly, allowing them to avoid being restricted to fixed roles and to fully capitalize on scoring opportunities.
column61:
Our software innovation is unified sequence control.
The algorithm adapts the best behavior based on the fusion data from multiple sensors, enabling it to determine which offence or defence robot approaches the goal and indicates the appropriate timing of initiating the shoot.
The color signature of each goal acquired by the omnidirectional camera plays an important role in our software, enabling the robot to find the non-blocked region of the opponent's goal and increase the scoring.

column15: External Nano caps motor-driver current (overheating); bulk cap stopped Ultrasonic PCB resets; IR smoothing + camera fixed IR's short range.
column16: We planned in Notion (every task assigned per member with a deadline), shared all PCB, 3D-design and code files on Google Drive, and ran a WhatsApp group for daily task lists and progress updates.
column17: We used Claude's Fusion 360 MCP to help design the supporting beams that hold the IR sensor, and to help lay out the Power PCB and Ultrasonic PCB
column32: Designed in Fusion 360 from hand sketches. With our physics teacher we compared 3- vs 4-omni-wheel layouts, wheel diameters and sensor placement within the 22 cm / 1.5 kg IR-league limits; the 4-wheel diamond won for holonomic agility and even traction. To improve it we added a 3D-printed support-beam frame that keeps the IR ring rigid and planar, sized the capture mouth to the 1.5 cm ball-zone limit, and packed the five PCBs into a compact stack.
column33: PCBs were made by JLCPCB; parts were 3D-printed on a Bambu Lab printer in PETG (TPU for grip). We tuned print tolerances, reamed the IR-cover holes to 3.2 mm, and reshaped the support beams to clear the Power-PCB terminals and Teensy USB.
column34: 4 motors, one per omni wheel in a diamond (Pololu 20D mm 12 V gearmotors). Four wheels give true holonomic motion - the robot drives straight at the ball from any bearing while the gyro holds heading - and spread force evenly for pushing duels. We 3D-printed our own omni wheels (PETG hubs + TPU rollers) so the diameter, roller count and grip fit our chassis and the smaller ball.
column35: A solenoid kicker in the capture mouth. The 3D-printed capture area cradles the ball within the 1.5 cm zone; a solenoid plunger strikes the ball forward. A printed mount and coupling align the plunger to the ball centre, and the kick only fires once the capture HC-SR04 confirms the ball is seated.
column36: N/A
column38:
Our proudest part is the fully self-designed, 3D-printed omni wheels. No off-the-shelf wheel fit our size limit or the smaller 2026 ball, so we built our own: PETG hubs with TPU-gripped rollers, refined over three print rounds to dial in roller diameter, count, grip and concentricity for smooth, slip-free holonomic motion. 
Second: a printed support-beam structure that ties the Main, IR-cover and ultrasonic boards into one rigid stack so the IR ring stays planar and aligned through collisions.
column41: A 12 V LiPo pack feeds a Power PCB which generates: 5 V (buck) for the Teensy boards boards and the OpenMV camera; 3.3 V (buck) for sensor logic; and 48 V (boost) for the solenoid kicker (All this is done on one PCB). The 12 V rail goes directly to the motor drivers. Bulk capacitors sit on each rail
column42: Each omni wheel has its own DRV8263H brushed driver (12 V power, 3.3 V logic, PH/EN mode); the Main Teensy feeds each a direction + PWM pin. Each driver's IPROPI current output goes to an Arduino Nano that pulls DRVOFF to coast a motor over ~1 A (stall ~1.6 A), preventing stall overheating.
column43:
3× Teensy 4.1:
(1) Main board: chosen for its 600 MHz speed and many hardware UARTs (it must service 5 serial links + the comms module at 100 Hz).
(2) IR ring board: needs to read 16 analog channels fast with on-chip averaging; 
(3) Line ring board: reads 18 analog channels at >500 Hz. 2×

Arduino Nano Every:
(4) Ultrasonic board: Receives values from 4 HC-SR04s; 
(5) Motor-current limiter: Current protection runs independently of the main code. 

Plus the OpenMV H7 as a vision coprocessor.
column44: The main Teensy 4.1 PWMs four DRV8263H drivers (one direction pin + one analogWrite PWM pin each, shared SLEEP, 12 V). It mixes a translation pattern with a gyro heading-correction term, so it strafes any direction while facing forward. A separate Nano caps current via IPROPI/DRVOFF.
column45: A 16-channel TSSP58038 IR receiver ring is the primary ball sensor - the IR-league ball emits IR, and each receiver's reading becomes an intensity (baseline - raw). An OpenMV H7 camera complements it: besides finding the goal, keeper and open-corner aim, it also detects the orange ball, letting us cross-check and reconfirm the IR ring's bearing - camera+IR fusion that helps at short range where the IR ring alone struggled. The camera sends its data to the main board as a 11-byte frame.
column46: A dedicated Line PCB carries four QTR-MD-05A IR-reflectance arrays - 18 sensors facing the floor on all sides. The white field line reflects differently from the green carpet (white reads low), and a board flags 'line' when >=2 of its channels cross threshold. The four ultrasonics range the walls.
column47: Direction: a BNO055 9-DOF IMU on the main board (I2C/Wire2, address 0x28) in IMUPLUS mode, so the drifting magnetometer is ignored and gyro+accel give a stable heading for the 100 Hz hold-PID. Position: four HC-SR04 ultrasonics, one per side, wired to the ultrasonic Nano that streams their distances to the main board for boundary and goal standoff.
column48: A relay module (IN1 = pin 22, active-HIGH) switches the 48 V solenoid; a capture HC-SR04 over the mouth (TRIG 20 / ECHO 21) confirms the ball before firing. Firmware safety: 2-sample capture debounce, a fixed 500 ms pulse, 1.5 s cooldown, a hard 600 ms max-on cutoff, and a boot-safe relay state.
column49: N/A
column52: Our federated multi-PCB architecture: three purpose-built boards talk over CRC-8-framed UART, so a glitch on any wire is rejected, not acted on. We are proudest of three boards. The Power PCB is compact yet generates and distributes four rails (12 V, 5 V, 3.3 V, 48 V) cleanly and continuously across the whole robot from one battery. The IR PCB gives each of its 16 receivers its own RC filter, smoothing the pulsed IR into a clean analog level the Teensy reads as a precise bearing. The Main PCB integrates everything on one board - it takes data from every sensor link, runs the heading PID, drives all four motors, and carries the BNO055 IMU and the solenoid kicker at once.
column54: The IR board reads all 16 channels, converts each to intensity = baseline - raw, rejects ambient and damps lone spikes. It then takes an intensity-weighted vector sum (atan2 over all lit sensors) for a smooth bearing, with hysteresis (60/35) and EWMA smoothing, and sends <dir>a<dist>b (500 = no ball) to the main board. The camera is read as an 11-byte frame; stale links (>200 ms) are ignored.
column55:
Attacker: it sorts the IR bearing into a drive sector and translates straight at the ball (speed 250) while the gyro holds heading; on capture it switches to aim-and-kick. 

Defender: it does not chase up-field - it stays on its goal line and runs a lateral PID on the ball's bearing to keep its body between ball and goal, with an intercept boost when the bearing moves fast, clearing on contact.
column56: The four ultrasonics are used reactively (no x/y estimate), each giving its wall distance. The attacker uses them with the line ring to flee field edges; the defender centres in the mouth from the left/right difference and holds a set distance-range from its own goal via the back sensor. Drive is holonomic: a translation vector plus the heading-PID correction, normalised so no wheel saturates.
column57: Walls are sensed by ultrasonics, the white boundary by four QTR-MD-05A reflectance arrays (>=2 white channels = line). Attacker: a side is the edge only when its ultrasonic reads <300 mm AND that side sees white, debounced 3 passes; it then flees, never on a stale link. This two-of-two rule stops a false reading triggering an out-of-bounds penalty. Defender: the line keeps it on the goal edge.
column58: The camera finds the goal and keeper and returns the open-corner bearing. On capture (mouth HC-SR04 <45 mm, 2 samples) the attacker holds heading, then spins in place until the open corner is within 5 deg ahead, and only then fires the 48 V solenoid (500 ms pulse, 1.5 s cooldown, 600 ms hard cutoff). The defender uses the same kicker for a short clearing kick. No dribbler.
column59: The defender's Defender_Full runs GUARD (centre on the line, hold standoff) → TRACK (lateral-PID onto the ball, intercept boost) → CLEAR (push/kick the ball away on contact) → RECOVER (re-home to the line). A back-wall safety rule forbids reversing toward its own goal when the rear wall is < 90 mm, so a bad reading can't drive it into its own net.
column60: Yes - the robots share status over a 2.4 GHz link (the only band RCJ permits for inter-robot comms). We use it for automatic role-switching: if one robot drops off the link (fouled, penalised or removed), its partner switches from attacker to defender so our goal is never left open while we are a robot down; they resume normal roles when it returns at kick-off.
column61:
Both are about clean data from cheap sensors.

(1) Ultrasonic smoothing: we ping opposite sensors together to kill cross-talk, temperature-compensate distance, run a per-sensor median-of-3, and coast over dropouts so a missed echo never blinks a wall away.

(2) IR tracking: instead of strongest-sensor, we sum intensity-weighted vectors over all lit receivers (atan2) with hysteresis and spike damping, for a smooth bearing on the small fast ball. Both feed our fused line+ultrasonic boundary escape

column15: Our mentor primarily contributed by managing our time and resources, helping us organize tasks, prioritize work, and keep the project on track.
column16: We divided the project into tasks and assigned them to team members. We used WhatsApp and Discord for communication, GitHub for code, Google Docs for documentation, and Google Sheets for task and schedule management.
column17: We used ChatGPT, Claude, and Perplexity to support our work throughout the project.
column32: We designed the robot in Fusion 360. Our main goal was to minimize weight while improving our previous robot design. We also tested different materials for the plow to find the best balance of durability and performance.
column33: We used 3D printers and silicone molds. Our PCBs were made by an external company from our own designs. We replaced the silicone dribbler with printer rubber rollers and refined the wheels and PCB layout for better performance.
column34:
We used a total of 5 motors. Four motors are used for movement, allowing the robot to move in all directions. One additional motor is used for the dribbler.

We designed and manufactured our own wheels to achieve better grip, make them easy to produce in-house, and customize their size to best fit our robot's design and performance requirements.
column35: Our robot uses a 6 V solenoid as the kicker mechanism. To achieve the required kicking power, the solenoid is powered by a 40 V boost converter.
column36: The dribbler uses a MAXON motor with GT2 pulleys driving a rubber roller. The mechanism is hinge-mounted and spring-loaded. When the ball enters, it lifts the dribbler, increasing pressure. A built-in switch detects the ball by being released when the ball is captured.
column38: We are most proud of significantly reducing the robot's weight while preparing the design to support two dribblers and two kickers. This was achieved by using a lightweight 4S LiHV battery, which is about half the weight of an equivalent LiPo battery, lightweight motors, and using two PCBs as the main structural frame of the robot.
column41: A 4S LiHV battery (14.4–17.4V) powers the drive motors. A boost converter generates 40V for the kicker, a buck converter provides 5V for LEDs and cameras, and a 3.3V LDO powers the microcontroller and sensors.
column42: We use MAXON ECX motors driven by ESCON 24/2 motor drivers. The motor drivers are controlled by the microcontroller using a digital direction pin, while the motor speed is regulated with a PWM signal.
column43: We use an ESP32-S3 microcontroller. We chose it because of its built-in Wi-Fi and ESP-NOW protocol, which enables fast communication between our robots. It also provides high performance and is easy to program and integrate into our system.
column44: We use high-level functions to calculate the robot's movement vector, which is stored in a motion buffer. At the end of each control cycle, low-level functions convert it into PWM values and directions for all four motors. These signals are then sent directly to the motor drivers.
column45: We use an IR ball sensor that provides the ball distance, angle, and raw values, which we process in software. Each robot has two cameras, both detecting both goals for field orientation. The front camera is mainly used for shooting, while the rear camera helps the defender with positioning and navigation.
column46: We use 32 LED/phototransistor pairs in an outer ring and 16 in an inner cross. Reflected light from the white line changes the phototransistor voltage. Three multiplexers read all sensors. The ring detects line angle, the cross detects line depth.
column47: We use a BNO055 gyroscope for heading and PixyCam v2.1 cameras for field navigation by detecting both goals. All sensors are connected to the ESP32-S3 via the I²C bus.
column48: The microcontroller controls a MOSFET driver, which switches a MOSFET to power the kicker solenoid. A flyback circuit protects the MOSFET when the solenoid is turned off.
column49: The dribbler is driven by a MAXON motor controlled by a MAXON ESCON 24/2 motor driver. The motor speed is regulated by the microcontroller using a PWM signal.
column52: We are most proud of our line detection circuit. By using addressable LEDs instead of standard white LEDs, we achieved more reliable and consistent line detection. We also improved the robot with a more powerful microcontroller, better motors, and a more efficient power circuit, resulting in higher overall performance and reliability.
column54: We detect the ball using an IR ball detection sensor. The sensor provides the ball direction, distance, and raw values. All data is read via I²C and processed by the microcontroller.
column55: The ball-catching algorithm depends on the robot's role. The defender stays near the goal line to protect the goal, while the attacker moves based on the angle between the robot and the ball. In general, both robots try to approach the ball with the front of the robot while minimizing rotation, as this gives us the best results.
column56: The attacker does not need an exact field position while searching for the ball. When it has the ball, it uses the cameras to detect both goals, move toward the opponent's goal, and find the best shooting position. The defender mainly uses the rear camera to detect and position itself in front of its own goal.
column57: We use 48 pairs of addressable LEDs and phototransistors for line detection. When a line is detected, the robot moves 180° away from it. The escape speed depends on how far the line is under the robot, measured by the inner cross of 18 LED/phototransistor pairs.
column58: When the attacker has the ball and sees the goal, it moves toward it, aligns to maximize the shooting angle, and shoots. The dribbler keeps the ball close to the robot for controlled movement. Once the robot has the ball, a clear view, and a good shooting angle, the kicker sends the ball into the goal.
column59: The defender stays near the goal area and always faces the ball to reduce the opponent's shooting angle and cover as much of the goal as possible. The attacker focuses on chasing the ball, which also helps disrupt the opponent and makes scoring more difficult.
column60: Yes, our robots communicate using ESP-NOW. They mainly use this communication to switch roles. When both robots are on the field, one acts as the defender and the other as the attacker, and they can swap roles to avoid blocking each other and improve teamwork.
column61: We are most proud of the communication between our robots. It allows them to switch roles dynamically, improving coordination, field awareness, and teamwork. This makes our gameplay more flexible and increases our chances of winning.

column15: ​Our mentor helped us refine our robot's movement strategy to perform more efficiently during matches.
column16: We used Notion to divide tasks among team members, refine schedules, and share and record the TDP production and development process in real-time. I also managed the code using GitHub.
column17: We used ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude to support brainstorming, algorithm comparison, and preparation of documentation.
column32: We designed compact holonomic robots that can move in any direction while controlling their heading. We considered size, weight, camera visibility, sensor placement, ball control, maintenance, center of mass, and protection from collisions.
column33: We assembled the robots using custom-made and commercially available parts. During the building process, we adjusted the structure, component positions, and wiring based on testing to improve reliability, maintenance, and overall performance.
column34: The robot uses four drive motors and one dribbler motor. Four drive motors allow holonomic movement in any direction.
column35: We designed a solenoid-based kicker. We optimized the spring mechanism and internal circuitry to achieve maximum force while maintaining a compact size.
column36: Our dribbler uses a high-RPM DC motor attached to a rubber-coated roller. We designed the housing to firmly hold the ball against the robot's body to maintain control during movement.
column38: The robust design provides excellent durability during intense matches. However, it has a maintenance issue where we have to disassemble the entire chassis for even minor repairs. Therefore, we plan a complete redesign after this competition to adopt a modular structure while maintaining the same durability
column41: The robots use an 11.1 V 3S Li-Po battery. Motor drivers use the battery supply directly, while regulated voltage is provided to the Arduino, camera, sensors, and display.
column42: The Arduino sends PWM and direction signals to separate motor drivers. Each motor is controlled independently. The software combines X movement, Y movement, and rotation, then converts them into individual wheel outputs.
column43: We use an Arduino Mega 2560-based controller because it has many I/O pins, timers, serial ports, and PWM outputs. An OpenMV H7 Plus handles image processing separately and sends processed data to the Arduino.
column44: The Arduino calculates X-axis movement, Y-axis movement, and rotational correction. These values are converted into four wheel commands. Compass feedback is used for heading control, and all outputs are limited before PWM is applied.
column45: The OpenMV H7 Plus detects the ball and goals using LAB color thresholds and blob filtering. It calculates object angle and distance, then sends the processed results to the Arduino through a fixed UART packet with a checksum.
column46: The robot uses 24 downward reflectance sensors, including 16 inner sensors and eight outer sensors. Their values are calibrated and thresholded. The positions of active sensors are combined to estimate the line direction.
column47: We use a BNO055 IMU for heading, four VL53L1X ToF sensors for wall distance, an OpenMV camera for the ball and goals, and 24 line sensors for the field boundary. We do not use wheel encoders.
column48: The Arduino controls a high-current switching circuit that powers the solenoid. A MOSFET or transistor handles the current, and a protection diode suppresses voltage spikes. The software forces the kicker off after about 300 ms.
column49: The dribbler motor is powered through a separate motor driver controlled by the Arduino. A rotating roller contacts the upper part of the ball and pulls it toward the robot during capture, movement, and shooting alignment.
column52: We separate vision processing and real-time control. The OpenMV processes images, while the Arduino controls motors and sensors. We also distinguish normal object loss from communication failure and use 24 sensors for detailed line detection.
column54: The OpenMV detects the ball using LAB color thresholds and filters blobs by size, shape, and position. It converts the selected blob into an angle and estimated distance, then sends the result to the Arduino through UART.
column55: The offensive robot combines a direct vector toward the ball with a tangential vector around it. This helps the robot move behind the ball instead of pushing it sideways. Near capture, sideways motion is reduced.
column56: The robots do not calculate a complete global position. They use IMU heading, goal direction, ToF wall distances, and line sensors to estimate only the information needed for each movement and defensive position.
column57: The software combines the positions of active line sensors into a line vector. It then calculates a safe escape direction and removes movement components that would push the robot farther outside the field.
column58: After capturing the ball, the offensive robot detects the opponent’s goal and calculates a target heading. The heading is filtered and rate-limited. The kicker fires only after ball capture and alignment conditions are satisfied.
column59: The defensive robot tracks the ball laterally and uses rear and side ToF sensors to stay near the center of its own goal. It moves to block the predicted ball path and can clear the ball during long deadlocks.
column60: No. The robots do not communicate with each other. Each robot makes decisions independently using its own camera, IMU, ToF sensors, and line sensors. Their offensive and defensive roles are fixed.
column61: Our main software innovation is combining direct ball approach and tangential orbit vectors with separately adjustable powers. We also use vector-based line escape, robust camera packets, heading filtering, and forced kicker shutdown.

column15: Our mentor gave feedback on last year’s design, helping us improve weaknesses while building from the existing base.
column16: We communicated through LINE where we assigned tasks daily and discussed tasks based on each member’s strengths. We tracked progress using a whiteboard which recorded contributions, design changes, testing results, and issues encountered.
column17: Codex assisted with code debugging. ChatGPT was used to generate regression equation for distance calibration and help with component selection.
column32:
Our previous dribbler (ball intake) used a gear-driven system. However, when it jammed or stalled, it could put excessive stress on the motor and potentially damage both the motor and the battery.

To improve reliability, we changed the design from gears to a synchronous timing belt system. In the event of a jam, the belt can slip or become loose, which helps absorb the overload instead of transferring the force back into the motor. This protects both the motor and the battery from damage.
column33: We used a 3D printer (BambuLab A1 Mini) and CNC machine (Makera Carvera) to make Fusion 360 parts. Some parts were outsourced for higher quality, and PCB designs were manufactured by JLCPCB. Small 3D-printed parts were reprinted during testing.
column34: We used 4 motors for the robot’s movement with omni wheels in a diagonal configuration. We chose a 4-motor setup because it is a common configuration in competition robots, as it offers a good amount of stability and torque. The use of omni wheels enables the robot to move smoothly in any direction without needing to rotate first.
column35: We found that thicker copper produced more power, but it also increased current and made the PCB prone to damage. We chose a copper thickness thicker than the original but not excessive for a balance between power and reliability. We also reduced solenoid power for safety.
column36: The dribbler uses a brushless motor connected to a silicone roller through a timing belt. The shield was CNC machined instead of 3D printed, improving strength and durability. We also used separate silicone sections, improving the dribbler’s stability.
column38:
Our previous dribbler (ball intake) used a gear-driven system. However, when it jammed or stalled, it could put excessive stress on the motor and potentially damage both the motor and the battery.

To improve reliability, we changed the design from gears to a synchronous timing belt system. In the event of a jam, the belt can slip or become loose, which helps absorb the overload instead of transferring the force back into the motor. This protects both the motor and the battery from damage.
column41: Our robot uses a 12V battery system. The motor drivers and ESC run directly from the 12V rail. A buck converter steps the voltage down for the microcontroller, while sensors and the IMU operate from a regulated 3.3V rail.
column42: The robot uses four DC motors, each driven by a DRV887x H-bridge motor driver. The Teensy controls the drivers using a phase-and-enable setup, sending PWM signals for speed control and direction signals to reverse motor rotation.
column43: We use a Teensy 4.1 as the main controller. We chose it for its fast processing speed, reliable PWM outputs, and real-time performance. It can simultaneously handle motor control, sensor processing, and communication, helping keep the robot responsive during operation.
column44: The Teensy 4.1 controls four DRV887x motor drivers by outputting PWM signals (0–255 via analogWrite) for speed and digital signals for direction. Each motor is assigned specific pins, and values from kinematic calculations are continuously updated to control omni-directional movement.
column45: Our robot detects the ball using an OpenMV camera running a custom vision script. It tracks objects in the Lab color space and uses calibrated thresholds to identify the orange ball, as well as yellow and blue goals. It calculates the target’s position relative to the image center to determine direction and approximate distance. This data is then sent to the Teensy 4.1 through a high-speed connection, allowing the robot to continuously track the ball and react to its movement during a match.
column46: Our robot uses a circular line sensor board with 18 LED–GL5506 photoresistor. A co-processor reads analog inputs (A0–A17), applies a rolling average, and uses EEPROM calibration to detect the white line. Position data is sent to the Teensy 4.1, which determines line position and returns in bounds.
column47: Our robot uses an Adafruit BNO055 IMU and an OpenMV camera for navigation, plus 3 LiDAR sensors for distance. The IMU connects to the Teensy 4.1 via I2C (100 Hz) to track heading. The OpenMV camera sends ball/goal positions via serial. Position is estimated by combining IMU heading with camera angles for field navigation.
column48: The kicker uses a high-current solenoid driven by a BTH500301 PROFET high-side switch. The Teensy 4.1 triggers it via digital pin 37, sending a 70 ms pulse to fire the solenoid. Built-in protection handles faults, and software enforces a 3s cooldown between kicks.
column49: The dribbler uses a brushless motor driven by an ESC controlled by the Teensy 4.1 via PWM. During a match, the teensy adjusts dribbler speed based on the robot state: high speed when chasing the ball to pull and hold it against the chassis, and slower when preparing to shoot to maintain possession.
column52: The part of our electronics that we are most proud of is our custom dual-PCB. Instead of placing every component on a single board, we designed two specialized PCBs: a main controller board and a circular bottom sensor board. One challenge we faced was that high-power devices such as the motor drivers, dribbler ESC, and kicker could introduce electrical noise that interferes with sensitive sensors. To solve this, we placed all 18 line sensors and their dedicated co-processor on the bottom board, allowing the sensor system to process readings locally before sending the data back to the main Teensy controller. This reduced wiring, simplified the PCB layout, and helped improve the reliability of our line detection system. On the main PCB, we also moved the motor drivers and kicker control circuitry directly onto the board, which reduced exposed wiring and made the robot easier to assemble and maintain. When the robot crosses the boundary line, the two center sensors detect the midpoint and allow the robot to move backward and recover its position. Since this was our first time designing a full main controller PCB, successfully integrating all these functions into a single board was a major achievement for our team. Overall, this design gave us a cleaner, more organized electrical system while improving both reliability and performance during competition.
column54: Our robot finds the ball using an OpenMV camera that detects the orange ball using calibrated color thresholds. When a valid target is detected, it calculates the ball’s position relative to the image center and sends the data via serial to the Teensy 4.1. The Teensy uses this to determine ball direction and distance in real time for gameplay response.
column55: Our ball-catching algorithm changes based on ball position. If the ball is in front, the robot drives straight toward it while using the dribbler to secure possession. If it is farther away, it adds a lateral offset to intercept smoothly. If close but misaligned, it loops behind the ball to re-approach toward the goal. Both robots use the same logic.
column56: Our robot estimates its field position by combining OpenMV camera data with BNO055 IMU heading. The IMU provides absolute orientation, while the camera gives relative positions of the ball and goals. This allows continuous tracking of field landmarks. If vision is lost, it uses the last known goal position to continue reliable navigation.
column57: Our robot uses a circular line sensor board with 18 GL5506 light sensors around the chassis. A co-processor reads the sensors, applies a 16-sample rolling average, and uses EEPROM calibration to detect boundary lines. When a line is detected, the center sensors locate the midpoint, and the robot moves backward to return inside the field.
column58: Our scoring algorithm first secures possession using the dribbler. If the ball is in front, the robot drives directly toward it. If farther away, it uses a lateral offset to intercept, and if misaligned, it moves behind the ball. Once in possession, the camera checks goal alignment and the Teensy activates the kicker when a clear shot is available.
column59: The robot uses a defensive routine to protect the goal line and prevent scoring. If the camera loses sight of the ball, it recenters using its own goal coordinates to stay in position. When the ball is visible, it calculates angular difference and moves laterally to block shots. If the ball stays in front for too long, it triggers a clear routine to push it out before returning to position.
column61: We use a greedy algorithm that selects the best immediate action based on the current game state. It evaluates available options and chooses the one with the highest short-term benefit, helping the robot make fast decisions during gameplay. This step-by-step approach reduces computation time and allows efficient decision-making using simple conditions.

column15: No mentor
column16: We used a Whatsapp group chat for communication and had a Notion to note down tasks and for organisation
column17: We used tools like Claude Code and GitHub Copilot to help write and debug code.
column32: We used Autodesk Fusion 360 to design custom 3D-printed components, including the dribbler pivot and LiDAR ring mount. Building on our seniors' design, we adapted the parts to better suit our robot while meeting competition size constraints. We also lengthened the dribbler pivot and added a support structure, improving its stability and enabling the dribbler to achieve more consistent possession and control of the ball.
column33: We used Bambu Studio to 3D print custom parts and sourced additional components from JLCPCB, JLCCNC, and JLCMC. We manually polished a blurry, unpolished concave mirror, improving its reflectivity and enabling more accurate ball detection.
column34: Our robot is powered by four JMP-BE-3560 motors, selected for their ability to provide high torque and precise control while maintaining fast acceleration. Using four independently driven motors also enables omnidirectional movement, allowing the robot to maneuver quickly and efficiently across the soccer field.
column35: Our robot uses a solenoid-powered kicker to deliver a rapid and consistent striking force. When activated, the solenoid drives a metal plunger forward to propel the ball. A 3D-printed kick plate increases the contact area with the ball, improving kicking consistency and scoring opportunities.
column36: Inspired by Hwa Chong's Transcendence team, our dribbler uses a brushless motor and a 1:4 gearbox to drive silicone-moulded rollers at 300 RPM. The rollers generate backspin, allowing the robot to securely gain and maintain possession while positioning the ball for passing or kicking.
column38: We are most proud of our 360° LiDAR ring, which consists of 20 LiDAR sensors mounted on the robot's top plate for localization. Using a rotating calipers algorithm, it accurately measures distances to the field boundaries. Even when some sensors are obstructed by other robots, the system maintains reliable positioning. This enables precise navigation, prevents the robot from going out of bounds, and supports accurate shooting and defensive positioning.
column41: Our robot uses 3S lithium polymer batteries that have a nominal voltage of 11.1V, but we usually use it between 12.6V (fully charged) and 11.5V for consistency. There are two buck converters, one down to 5V and the other down to 3.3V, for different microcontrollers and sensors.
column42: We use 4 DRV8871 motor drivers, which are commercially available and affordable motor drivers. We use them to supply up to 12V from the battery to the motors. They are controlled using PWM signals from the ESP32-S3 on the bottom PCB.
column43: We use an ESP32-P4-WIFI6 on the main PCB (the middle PCB). We chose it as it has four usable hardware UART controllers, which allows us to use the UART protocol to connect to our other boards (top, bottom and OpenMV). It is also a powerful and modern (less than 1 year since release) microcontroller. On the top and bottom PCBs, we use an ESP32-S3-Zero on each, as they are cheap, adequately fast, and have 2.4GHz wireless communication built-in which we use for bot-to-bot communication.
column44: An ESP32-S3 sends PWM signals to four DRV8871 motor drivers, which control the motors via XT30 connectors. We calibrated the motors by balancing opposite wheel pairs at different speeds, determining compensation values to improve movement accuracy and consistency.
column45: We use an OpenMV Cam H7 R2 pointing at a distortion-free mirror. We use the find_blobs() function in OpenMV’s MicroPython to find an orange blob in the image according to a threshold.
column46: We don’t detect the lines at all. We just use our highly accurate and reliable LiDAR-based localisation system to find out where we are on the field.
column47: A BNO085 IMU on the top PCB tracks the robot's rotation about its vertical axis. The ESP32-S3 reads the IMU data (via UART) and transmits it, together with LiDAR-based localization data, to the ESP32-P4 on the middle PCB. The ESP32-P4 uses PID control to maintain the robot's intended heading, enabling stable and accurate navigation.
column48: We use a solenoid, and the kicker is connected to the kick plate.
column49: Our dribbler silicone rollers are connected to 4 gears, with gear ratio 1:4, which is then connected to the brushless motor we use. We use rubber bands as extensions to keep the dribbler in place
column52: Our custom 360° LiDAR ring uses 20 sensors to localize the robot by measuring distances to the field boundaries. A rotating calipers algorithm provides accurate positioning even when some sensors are blocked, enabling reliable navigation, precise positioning, and preventing the robot from going out of bounds.
column54: A camera mounted beneath a convex mirror captures a 360° view of the field in a single image. The robot detects the ball using colour filtering and blob detection, then estimates its distance from the robot based on its pixel position in the camera frame. This information is sent to the main microcontroller for navigation and ball tracking.
column55: The robot captures the ball by driving towards it while simultaneously rotating to face its direction. As it approaches, the robot slows down for greater accuracy and further reduces its speed when the heading differs significantly from the ball's direction, prioritizing alignment. Once the ball-capture LiDAR sensor is triggered, the robot transitions to the shooting stage.
column56: A ring of 20 time-of-flight LiDAR sensors measures distances to the field walls for localization. The readings are processed into a point cloud, from which the robot's position is calculated. Combined with IMU heading data, this provides accurate localization, while PID controllers use the position to navigate precisely to target locations across the field.
column57: LiDAR-based localization keeps the robot within the field boundaries. As the robot nears a boundary, a speed limit is applied based on its distance from the line. If exceeded, the commanded speed is reduced proportionally. At the boundary, a corrective velocity away from the wall is applied, preventing the robot from moving out of bounds.
column58: The striker captures the ball with its dribbler, then carries it toward the opponent's goal while avoiding defenders and maintaining alignment for stable ball control. Once within shooting range, it identifies an open gap in the goal, aims accordingly, and uses its solenoid kicker to shoot. The dribbler ensures secure possession throughout the process.
column59: The goalkeeper positions itself between the ball and the goal to minimize the visible scoring angle. If a direct path risks contacting the ball, it safely navigates around it instead. When the ball is not visible, it tracks the nearest opponent. If the ball remains stationary in its half, the goalkeeper switches to capture mode and secures possession.
column61: The localisation of the bot comms.
