column15: The movement algorithm planning for offense and defense (that unfortunately in the end didn't work correctly and we ran out of time to fully debug)
column16: Created a list of tasks for each person at each meeting that we had (online and in person) to divide up the work and work efficiently.
column17: We utilized Claude and Codex to help us debug our code, algorithm development support and software/hardware advice
column32: Our team uses Onshape as our primary CAD software. Our design aimed to prioritize fitting all the features we wanted to have. Despite reusing last year's design as a base, we needed to include new features such as LiDAR and a touch screen which posed huge space constraints. This means that we were forced to create tighter tolerances in CAD that were difficult to replicate in the real world.
column33:
Our entire design is fully 3D printed so revisions could be made quickly and cheaply. Our mirrors, however, were machined with aluminum by Xometry and our PCBS manufactured by JLCPCB.  
We had to edit the LCD mount to create space for LiDAR.
column34: Our team uses the Maxon DCX 19 Motors for the drivebase because they are sensitive to low voltage changes meaning we still retain control at low speeds. Our omniwheels are also custom 3D printed using cheap rubber rollers allowing us to create omniwheels that are much thinner than any product that we could find. We also use the RS19 for our dribbler motor due to its high speed.
column35: The Kicker is custom winded with a new wire gauge in order to increase the turn density of the solenoid. This increased turn density allows for the solenoid to create a greater magnetic field, which in turn, creates a harder kick.
column36: The dribbler was designed to be small while maintaining a necessary gear reduction. Our gear reduction was ~2:1 which allowed for our very high RPM motor to still have adequate torque to bring the ball up to speed while still spinning it extremely fast.
column38: A few key parts are our custom dribbler, custom solenoid, and custom mirror/tubing. Our dribbler was custom made to include a 2:1 gear reduction - ensuring that it has adequate speed and torque to spin the ball. Our custom solenoid was made with a much greater turn density than the stock solenoid to create a stronger kick. And, our mirror was made custom to allow for a smoother polish and more control over exactly what the robot sees.
column41:
Our robot has a 11.1V battery pack that is regulated to 3.3V for each of our teensies and 5V for our Arduino 
Voltage regulator in between to drop the 5V signal from the Arduino to 3.3V for the Teensy. Boost convertor to boost to 48V for our kicker circuit (more solenoid power)
column42: To drive our Maxon motors we utilized VNH7070ASTR Motor drivers which are connected to digital and analog pins on the teensy.
column43:
We utilized a teensy (which we call our main teensy) which sends commands to all our motors, kicker and controls state machine
We utilized another teensy specifically to communicate with our line pcb which had all of our line sensors
We utilized an Arduino to interface with an LCD screen
We utilized a PI to interface with the camera algorithm and our lidar
column44: Depending on the direction we want for each of the motors, we set the in1 and in2 pins (to HIGH or LOW) by using a digitalWrite to the main teensy pin that the motor drivers are connected to
column45: We utilize an Arducam IMX708 which directly connects to the Pi 5 that we have via the SPI interface
column46: We utilize photoelectric sensors, LEDs and resistors on our LinePCB. We have 48 of the Photoelectric sensors and LEDs and 96 resistors and each of the sensors is connected to one of 8 channels on 6 ADCs. Each ADC corresponds to pins on the teensy (with a chip select pin and then mosi, miso, sck)
column47:
Navigation - Lidar + Camera algorithm (heading of the goals & ball)
Field Position - Lidar
Direction Robot Faces - BNO055 IMU
column48: Our kicker system is driven by a system of P and N-channel MOSFETS to safely isolate our boost converter and discharge our capacitors. The P-Channel isolates the high side of the circuit through a voltage divider and an optocoupler while the N-Channel safely allows discharges through the solenoid.
column49: Our dribbler system utilizes an RS19 controller by a VNH7070ASTR and a 2:1 gear reduction to achieve a reasonable torque to have control of the ball.
column52: The achievement we're most proud of electrically is figuring out how to implement the Raspberry Pi Compute Module 5 into our PCB. The electrical design required that we learn how to route differential pairs, calculate impedance, and plan for high frequency EMI in our PCB. Surprisingly this innovation had worked with out first batch of PCBs including the compute module and all of peripherals had functioned as to plan. During the process we had to learn a lot about professional PCB: with research taking over a month.
column54: We utilize the Picamera2 library to recieve frames from the camera and then based on our hsv thresholds, we are able to find the center coordinates of the deteced ball and utilize an inverse tangent function to figure out the ball angle based on pixel distance from center. We utilize a trained Neural Network to find distance to the ball.
column55: Our robot uses an orbit algorithm which commands the robot to move at the ball angle + an orbit angle calculated by an exponential function that utilizes distance to the ball and the angle to the ball in order to perfectly get behind the ball. Offense utilizes orbit while defense takes the vector projection of the ball angle onto left and right vectors and a heading vector normal to the goal line.
column56: We utilize the the values we retrieve from the lidar to be able to localize well by finding rays/clusters of points along a minimum of 2 walls. We then use that to path plan to specific poses on the field for our spin shots.
column57: We have our own custom line avoidance algorithm that that figures out which sensors have been activated on the line based on pre-calibrated values, utilizes coordinate geometry of the placement of the sensors and PCA to calculate the intersection angle with the line. Then our robot moves in the opposite direction of the line intersection angle.
column58:
We have 2 methods to score goals implemented with state machines. 
For spin shots we path to a pre-selected pose on the field with our dribbler activated and then spin and then once our robot is within a threshold of the angle facing the goal then the kicker kicks the ball.
Normal shot: When moving to the ball we would always face the goal - no dribbler - once we have ball we shoot
column59: Our robot tries to "line follow" on the goalie line and rotate our heading towards where the ball is. Our robot moves in an angle relatively staying close to the line/on the line based on the predicted intersection of the line from the ball to the center of the goal and the goalie line.
column60: Our robot's utilize bluetooth to communicate with each other. When one robot is off then whichever one is in the field temporarily becomes the offense/goal scoring robot as if the other robot is off that means it got taken out of game play.
column61: We’re most proud of our BallAlgo and LiDAR localization. BallAlgo uses the ball’s recent movement to predict its next camera sector, so we search a small area instead of the whole image, making detection much faster. Our LiDAR system uses many wall points and Hough-style line voting to estimate the robot’s field position more reliably than ultrasonic sensors. Together, they help us react faster, attack smarter, and win more games.

column16: We use Whatsapp and Discord for comunication, YouTrack and a Discord server for assigning tasks and a self hosted Gitlab server for version control.
column17: We used ChatGPT and Claude Code for debugging and research.
column32: For designing our robot we use Fusion360 and Eagle. During the development we tried to make the assembly as easy and fast as possible so we can swap broken components between matches. Another choice related to time efficiency is using two batteries so we can hotswap them without restarting the Raspi every single time. Furthermore we used several types of filament for different requirements, e.g. the dribbler gears produced enough heat to melt their mount printed with PETG so we switched to PC.
column33:
Structural parts: 3D-printed with either TPU, PETG or PC
Omniwheels: CNC-milled by a local partner
360° hyperbolic mirror: custom-made externally
PCBs: manufactured by JLCPCB
silicone dribbler rollers: cast in 3D-printed moulds and cured under vacuum
column34: We use 4 Faulhaber 3216BXTH BLDC motors with planetary gearboxes, as an optimal balance between space and grip: 5 motors wouldn't fit and 3 couldn't provide enough grip. To improve the grip even further we sitched from O-profile to X-profile wheels for more contact area.
column35: First we bought several Solenoids from Tremba and tested the power to size ratio at different voltages. Then we took the best one and rewound it, using orthocyclic windings and a thinner wire. This allowed us to maximize the total amount of windings and therefore increase the power.
column36: We use gears which cause a lot of concentrated heat. To prevent the mount from melting we use PC filament. For achieving an optimal compromise between grip and durability we tested several types of silicone from Silikonfabrik with different stiffness. The motor is a AT208 drone motor from T-motor.
column38: We use a double LiDAR system to minimize our blind-spots so we can get an accurate position of the robot. With a mouse sensor we double check the position to reduce our margin of errors. To save us some time during testing we use two batteries which we can swap individually so we don't have to restart he Raspi and re-calibrate our sensors every single time.
column41: The robot is powered by two 14.8V batteries. Their outputs are combined through ideal diodes before reaching the regulation stage. Three linear regulators provide 3.3 V and two 5 V rails: one for the Raspberry Pi, and one for miscellaneous sensors. The kicker uses a boost converter to generate 48 V.
column42: Our Teensy outputs a digital signal for direction and a PWM signal for speed that gets converted to an analog signal via a series of resistors and capacitors. Both get measured by our Escon-24/2 motor drivers which then generates the necessary waveforms to drive our brushless motors.
column43: For camera data processing we use a Raspberry Pi CM4, because it is rather cheap and has a small form factor. The main micro controller we use for converting sensor data into drive instructions is the teensy 4.0. It is really fast especially for its price and size, featuring an ARM Cortex-M7 at 600MHZ.
column44: We calculate the direction and speed for each motor depending on the drive angle and speed that we want to drive. Then we add a PID controller to maintain our desired orientation. The resulting speed and direction get sent to the motor drivers through a digital and a PWM signal.
column45: A raspiCam HQ 12mp is connected to our raspi CM4. The camera looks upwards into a hyperbolic mirror to get a 360° view. We use a python script with openCV to detect the ball. This data is sent to the teensy, where it is further processed.
column46: We power 40 LEDs and measure the reflected light using photo transistors. Their signal is filtered by a capacitor and then measured by an ADC unit from which we read the signal voltage with our Teensy. The white line tape reflects more light, causing a higher voltage, allowing us to detect the line.
column47: We determine our direction using a BNO055 IMU. Our position on the field is calculated by the Raspberry Pi, which processes the distance measurements from our two LiDAR sensors. Since the LiDARs have a relatively low update rate, we fill in the gaps using the PMW3389 mouse sensor. The PMW3389 is connected to the Teensy via SPI, while the LiDARs and Raspberry Pi communicate over UART.
column48: A boost converter charges multiple capacitors to 48V. The kicker is activated through an N-channel MOSFET. A flyback diode protects the circuit from voltage spikes generated when the kicker is switched off while a timer prevents the solenoid from being activated for extended periods.
column49: We use a MCF8316C-Q1 BLDC motor driver to run our AT2308 drone motor that turns our silicone drum via three gears. In case the driver overheats we also have connectors for a common drone ESC.
column52: Due to the limited available space, we designed a 6-layer PCB to fit all required electronics. The additional layers allow better power distribution, cleaner signal paths, and a more compact design. We also implemented an ideal diode circuit for our two-battery system, enabling efficient power switching while preventing current flow between the batteries.
column54: We use OpenCV to search for contours in a binary image filtered by hsv thresholds. We use a function that's fitted to the curvature of the mirror to calculate the distance to the robot. Since the center of the Robot is in the center of the image we can simply use atan2 to get the corresponding angle.
column55:
The robot steers toward the ball using its camera angle, adding a offset that grows as the ball nears, so it curves in to scoop it rather than kicking it away, all while a PID keeps it facing the goal and the dribbler holds the ball. 
Both robots share identical firmware; the only difference is role: the attacker chases the ball, the defender stays on its goal line and intercepts.
column56: Using the BNO's heading we rotate the LiDAR scan to be axis-aligned, then measure the distance to all four walls. For each axis we check whether the two opposite distances sum up to the known field size within a small tolerance. With that information we can now drive to any given position on the field, by calculating the angle to that point using atan2, aswell as a distance for speed.
column57: We compare the values of 40 sensors to their calibrated detection threshold. Then, we add the sine and cosine values of each active sensor and calculate the line angle using atan2(). Once a line is detected, we use the calculated angle to drive away from it. When driving over the line, the calculated angle points in the wrong direction, so we need to correct it to keep driving back into the field.
column58: The robot always tracks the direction to the opponent's goal. When it reaches the ball it runs the dribbler to pull it into the front opening and hold it, then turns to face the goal using a PID. It fires the kicker only once the ball is secured and its heading is locked on the goal, so the shot goes off the instant it has a clean line; otherwise the kicker stays idle.
column59: We use a dynamic role-switching system that assigns one robot the role of defender. The defender positions itself between the ball and the center of the goal. Additionally, we designed the defender to move away from the goal rather than remain directly in front of it. This allows the robot to obscure a larger portion of the goal, making it more difficult for the opponent to score.
column60: Our robots communicate using an HC05 Bluetooth Module with a Bluetooth Classic connection. We send the positions of the robots, ball, and enemy to the other robot. This allows the use of a dynamic role switch based on both robots' and the ball's positions, allowing the best-positioned robot to attack. It also lets the defender defend the ball without seeing it, as long as its partner detects it.
column61:
We are most proud of our dual LiDAR setup. We use two LiDAR sensors and fuse their data in our code to achieve more accurate position detection with less interference and fewer blind spots. This makes our positioning on the field more reliable.
Our most recent advancement is sharing the ball position via Bluetooth, which enables the defending robot to defend the goal even when the ball is not visible, as long as the other robot can see it.

column11: Usually once a week after school, but more frequently closer to the competition.
column12: School robotics room, occasionally at one of our homes
column13: The start of the school year (around February)
column14: We’ve all competed in RCJ Australia at the national and state levels in both Standard (LEGO) and Open (Vision) divisions. Brendan was also part of the Vision (formerly Open) team that went to Brazil in 2025.
column15: Electronic part selection
column16: Each team member specialised in one aspect of robot design, so that tasks could be completed efficiently, while maintaining a shallow knowledge of other aspects to allow for efficient integration between different systems.
column17: We used ChatGPT, Github Copilot and Cursor to assist with coding.
column32: We used Fusion360 to design our mechanical parts. Some designs were based on our designs from previous years and inspired by other teams’ TDPs and posters from previous years. However, with the size limit decreased from our national competition and having no PCBs, we had to develop creative solutions to fit components.
column33: 3D-printed the non-electronic parts, and each circuit was hand soldered on Perfboards.
column34: 5 motors (four for locomotion, one for the dribbler). This provides enough force and grip to move the robot effectively without taking up excessive space.
column35: The kicker utilises a 5V solenoid that receives power from 3x 4700uF capacitors that are charged by a 40v boost converter. To increase kicking power, we selected a solenoid that is rated below the voltage we are providing it, as this means that more current would flow, increasing kicking power.
column36: The drum was cast with moulding silicone / silicone sealant. The squishiness of the silicone is usually enough to prevent the ball from rebounding. The parts (layout, gear system) were designed in fusion.
column38:
For our dribbler, a plain cylindrical / elliptical drum was chosen over other designs for a few reasons:
Methods of centring the ball using patterns on the drum are generally ineffective, instead the shape of the ball catching zone is used to centre the ball/
Wedge shaped dribblers have a smaller catching range and can also have anti-ball centering properties - when the ball moves to one side, the wedge spins the ball angled to that side and causes it to become stuck to that side.
column41: The 4S battery’s 14.8V is the main source of power. From there, the battery power is split between all other modules. The boost converter boosts the voltage to 40V for our kicking circuit, while the buck converter decreases the voltage to 5V.
column42: Each motor is connected to a Steel Bar Powerful Brushless Motor Driver v4. This motor receives instructions from the Raspberry Pi through I2C, then runs a PID loop to control motor voltages.
column43:
The robot is centred around a Raspberry Pi 5 8GB, which was originally chosen for its high processing power and ease of use via SSH.
The MaixCAM Lite modules each have a Sipeed LicheeRV Nano board, and were chosen due to their ability to run AI models from their Neural Processing Units (NPUs). We chose the MaixCAM specifically because they were fairly affordable and compact.
column44: The Raspberry Pi 5 communicates to the motor drivers through I2C which then controls the voltage and current sent to the motors. We decided to set the motor drivers to speed mode, which means the Raspberry Pi requests a certain speed and the motor drivers will try to maintain that speed.
column45: We are using 4 MaxiCam Lite cameras arranged around our robot to see the field. Each of these cameras comes with a small board with an Neural Processing Unit (NPU), which can handle running an AI model which will select the ball and the goals. This model was trained locally on a GPU by using a hand labelled dataset.
column46: N/A, see how we stay inside the lines below
column47:
We have a BNO085 IMU which is used to calculate the heading of the robot. The robot will always start facing in the direction it needs to score towards, so it saves this orientation as forwards.
We use time-flight-sensors to measure the robot’s distance from walls, combined with the angle and distance towards goals from the camera to estimate its position in the field.
column48: Our kicker relies on a 5V solenoid, which is designed to fire with the same force as its 12V equivalent, but at a lower voltage. Combining this with 40V from the boost converter and 14.1mF of capacitance allows our kicks to be much more powerful than if we had continued using a 12V solenoid.
column49: The dribbler is driven using the same motor and system as the movement motors. A gear reduction of roughly 8:1 is applied (after the builtin motor gearbox). This also serves to transmit power to the drum, as directly attaching our large motor didn’t fit.
column52:
The kicker circuit, which is described in detail in a previous question, as well as the choice of solenoid, are the most significant electrical innovations in our bots. 

The largest difference in this design from previous designs is the choice of optocoupler, which was chosen to minimise switching time for slightly better thermal performance in the MOSFET, due to its low rise, fall, and delay times, as well as its ability to drive battery voltage with sufficient current when powered via logic voltage. 

This reduces the time the MOSFET spends in its linear region to reduce heat loss, as the MOSFET will have 40V at around 30A flowing through it, due to the low voltage solenoid chosen.
column54: Each of our MaixCams have a custom YOLO26 detection model which is trained on over 1000 hand annotated images in Label Studio to be able to identify the ball, as well as both goals. The model returns a rectangle around the object which is then sent to the Raspberry Pi through UART, using an efficient protocol we designed.
column55: The robot adjusts its speed based on the ball’s movement. If the distance to the ball isn’t closing as expected, the ball is moving away from the robot, so it speeds up to catch it. If the ball is becoming closer than expected, it is moving towards the robot, so it slows down to avoid overshooting.
column56: The robot doesn’t know exactly where it is within the field, however it combines multiple data points to allow it to make good decisions about movement. Most of the time, at least one of the cameras can see at least one goal, which it can use to line up its shot. Additionally, the time of flight sensors are used to find how close the nearest wall is, so that it doesn’t ever hit it.
column57: We primarily use the four time-of-flight distance sensors mounted on the robot to measure the distance to the wall, and stop moving in that direction if a wall is too close. Moreover, to avoid fully entering the penalty area, the robot uses its distance from the goal, calculated by the MaixCams.
column58: Once the attacker gains possession, the robot chooses between simply moving towards the goal or hiding the ball along the side wall - whichever is more convenient. It then aligns itself with the goal and fires the kicker, taking a shot at the goal. Additionally, the robot can attempt long-range shots, utilising our powerful kicker to its full potential.
column59: Our defending strategy is to stay along the penalty area line and move to stay inline with the ball and the goal. If a ball is very close to the robot, it attempts to capture the ball and attack, swapping roles with the other robot.
column60: Our robots use WiFi to communicate with each other. We use this to ensure there is always at least one robot defending the goal while keeping roles flexible. This means that if the goalie blocks the goal, it can run across the field to score while the other robot drops back to goalie.
column61: We used MaixHub to develop an AI vision model to locate field objects such as the goals and ball more efficiently than with standard edge detection algorithms. This allows the tickrate of the robot to be faster, allowing it to respond faster to changing field conditions and chase and block the ball more effectively. This also means that the robot won’t be tricked by any other object with a similar colour in its field of view as it is specifically trained on images of the goals and balls.

column54: An IR Seeker detects the active IR ball over I2C, giving a clock direction (12 = front) and a strength value (smaller = closer); we convert the direction to an angle each loop. A Pixy2 adds a second source, reporting each object's image position and size and identifying the ball by color. The IR sees all around; the camera is more precise but narrower.
column55: The attacker never drives straight at the ball, which would knock it the wrong way. It adds a sideways component that grows with the ball's offset from front, curving around it until the ball sits between robot and goal. The defender is the opposite: it never collects the ball, it stays in front of it to block.
column56: Instead of exact coordinates, we fuse relative references: the BNO055 IMU gives an absolute heading (a fixed "forward"), the Pixy2 gives the goal's bearing, and the IR Seeker gives the ball's bearing. From these the robot always knows its orientation and where to go. Our omni drive then moves in any direction while holding the chosen heading.
column57: Analog floor sensors around the robot read surface brightness against a calibrated threshold to detect the white line. When sensors trigger, we take the vector sum of their mounting angles to find the line's direction, then drive the opposite way to escape. This line-escape is the highest priority, overriding everything else.
column58: We orbit the ball until it's directly ahead while keeping the robot aimed at the goal with the Pixy2 (IMU as fallback). The dribbler, a brushless motor with backspin, holds the ball at the front. With no possession sensor, we infer we have it when the IR Seeker reads it close and ahead, then drive forward and fire the solenoid near the goal.
column59: The defender's Pixy2 faces backward to see our own goal while the IR Seeker tracks the ball. It continuously positions itself on the line between ball and goal, becoming a moving wall that blocks any shot. We hold this line close to our goal to cover the widest angle, and treat the goal-area line as a hard boundary.
column60: No — each robot runs fully independently on its own sensors. This is deliberate: fixed roles (one attacker, one defender) make their behaviors complement each other implicitly, so they cover different areas without interfering. It's also more robust, with no link to fail. A wireless link to swap roles is a future option.
column61: Our proudest work is a shared omni-drive function (wheels at 60/120/240/300°) taking direction, speed and rotation, plus a minimum-power floor that fixed "spinning but stuck" motors. On top sits our ball-orbiting algorithm: born from watching the robot hit the ball the wrong way, it curves around the ball until it's between robot and goal, turning contacts into shots on goal.
